Policy iteration, introduced by Ronald Howard in 1960, alternates between two steps: policy evaluation (computing the value function for a given policy by solving a system of linear equations) and policy improvement (updating the policy to be greedy with respect to the current value function). Policy iteration converges in a finite number of steps for finite MDPs and is often faster in practice than value iteration, though each iteration is more expensive. Both algorithms require enumeration of the full state space, making them exact but limited to problems with at most a few million states. The continuous-time analogue of the Bellman equation is the Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman (HJB) equation, a partial differential equation that extends the classical Hamilton-Jacobi equation of mechanics to stochastic optimal control. Where the discrete Bellman equation maximises over a sum, the HJB equation maximises over a differential operator, yielding a PDE whose solution — the value function — gives the optimal cost-to-go at every point in a continuous state space. The HJB equation is central to financial mathematics (where it governs optimal portfolio allocation under the Merton model), to aerospace engineering (where it governs optimal trajectory design), and to the theory of viscosity solutions developed by Michael Crandall and Pierre-Louis Lions, for which Lions shared the 2004 Fields Medal. Key milestones in the development of stochastic dynamic programming, from Bellman's original RAND formulation through the modern convergence with reinforcement learning. The 1990s mark the critical bridge: Bertsekas and Tsitsiklis's Neuro-Dynamic Programming (1996) and Sutton and Barto's Reinforcement Learning (1998) translated the same ideas into the vocabularies of two different communities. The textbook formalisation of stochastic dynamic programming for the operations research community was completed by Martin Puterman's 1994 Markov Decision Processes: Discrete Stochastic Dynamic Programming, which remains the standard reference. Puterman, a professor at the University of British Columbia, provided the rigorous mathematical treatment — existence and uniqueness of optimal policies, convergence proofs for value and policy iteration, structural results on the form of optimal policies — that transformed dynamic programming from a collection of techniques into a self-contained mathematical theory. His treatment of the average-reward criterion, constrained MDPs, and partially observable MDPs (POMDPs) set the agenda for a generation of researchers. But it was Dimitri Bertsekas at MIT who would become the field's most influential living figure. Bertsekas, who earned his PhD at MIT in 1971 and has been on the faculty since 1979, published the first edition of Dynamic Programming and Optimal Control in 1978 — a two-volume work that has been revised and expanded four times and is now the principal textbook and reference for the field. The second volume, focused on approximate dynamic programming, runs to over 700 pages and covers the territory that connects classical DP to modern reinforcement learning. Bertsekas received the 2014 Richard E. Bellman Control Heritage Award, the 2014 Khachiyan Prize for lifetime accomplishments in optimisation, the 2015 George B. Dantzig Prize, and the 2018 John von Neumann Theory Prize (jointly with John Tsitsiklis). With Tsitsiklis, he co-authored Neuro-Dynamic Programming (1996), a monograph that established the theoretical foundations for using neural networks to approximate value functions — the idea that would, two decades later, power DeepMind's Atari-playing agents and AlphaGo. Warren Powell and the Three Curses At Princeton, Warren B. Powell — professor of operations research for 39 years and founder of CASTLE Laboratory — approached dynamic programming from the perspective of large-scale industrial applications: fleet management, energy systems, logistics. His 2011 book Approximate Dynamic Programming: Solving the Curses of Dimensionality identified not one but three curses: the curse of the state space (too many states to enumerate), the curse of the outcome space (too many stochastic outcomes to evaluate expectations), and the curse of the action space (too many decisions to optimise over). Powell's framework organised approximate methods into four classes of policies: myopic policies, lookahead policies, policy function approximations, and policies based on value function approximations. His 2022 book Reinforcement Learning and Stochastic Optimization proposed a unified framework for sequential decision problems that spans active learning, stochastic search, simulation optimisation, and dynamic resource allocation — arguing that "reinforcement learning" and "stochastic dynamic programming" are different names for overlapping but non-identical problem classes. The curse of dimensionality visualised. With 10 sample points per dimension, the number of grid points required to uniformly cover the state space grows exponentially. By 20 dimensions — a modest number for real-world control problems — the grid exceeds 10²⁰ points, rendering exact dynamic programming infeasible. The arsenal of weapons developed against the curse of dimensionality is now vast. Function approximation replaces the tabular value function — which stores a separate number for every state — with a parameterised function (linear in basis functions, polynomial, neural network) that generalises across states. State aggregation groups similar states together, reducing the effective state space. Simulation-based methods replace the exact expectation in the Bellman equation with sample averages, avoiding the need to enumerate all possible outcomes. Temporal difference learning, introduced by Richard Sutton in 1988 and rooted in the earlier work of Arthur Samuel (1959) on self-playing checkers programs, updates value estimates incrementally from experience rather than requiring full backward passes. A taxonomy of solution methods for stochastic dynamic programming, organised by computational strategy. Exact methods (blue) require enumeration of the full state space. Approximation methods (green) replace the value function with a parameterised model. Simulation-based methods (orange) learn from sampled trajectories. Decomposition methods (purple) exploit problem structure to break the problem into manageable pieces. The bridge between stochastic dynamic programming and reinforcement learning was built in the 1990s, principally by three books: Bertsekas and Tsitsiklis's Neuro-Dynamic Programming (1996), Sutton and Barto's Reinforcement Learning: An Introduction (1998), and Csaba Szepesvári's Algorithms for Reinforcement Learning (2010). The first brought the rigour of control theory and operations research; the second brought the intuition and vocabulary of psychology-inspired learning; the third brought the modern computational complexity perspective. The terminological confusion that resulted — "approximate dynamic programming" in operations research, "neuro-dynamic programming" in control, "reinforcement learning" in computer science, "optimal control" in engineering, "dynamic stochastic general equilibrium" in macroeconomics — reflects a genuine intellectual convergence: these communities were, and still are, solving the same Bellman equation with different tools and different priorities. The deep reinforcement learning revolution, catalysed by Mnih and colleagues' 2015 Nature paper on Deep Q-Networks (DQN) and consolidated by Silver and colleagues' AlphaGo (2016) and AlphaZero (2017), demonstrated that neural network function approximation could crack problems that had been considered intractable — Atari games from raw pixels, the game of Go with its 10^170^ possible board positions. These systems are, at their mathematical core, approximate solutions to the Bellman equation, implemented with convolutional and recurrent neural networks replacing the tabular value functions and linear basis functions of classical ADP. The continuity is real: Bertsekas's 2019 textbook Reinforcement Learning and Optimal Control presents deep RL as the latest chapter in a story that begins with Bellman in 1953. Stochastic Dual Dynamic Programming (SDDP) For problems with convex structure and stage-wise independence — common in energy systems planning, water resource management, and financial optimisation — Stochastic Dual Dynamic Programming, introduced by Mario Pereira and Leontina Pinto in 1991, provides a powerful decomposition approach. SDDP approximates the expected cost-to-go function with a piecewise-linear lower bound constructed from Benders cuts, which are generated by solving linear programs at sampled states and propagating dual information backward through the stages. The method scales to problems with thousands of state variables and hundreds of time stages — far beyond the reach of grid-based DP — and has been deployed operationally in the Brazilian electricity sector, where it helps plan the dispatch of hydroelectric and thermal generation under uncertain rainfall. Extensions to risk-averse and distributionally robust settings (using conditional value-at-risk or Wasserstein ambiguity sets) have been developed by Philpott, de Matos, and Shapiro. The applications of stochastic dynamic programming span almost every domain where sequential decisions meet uncertainty. In inventory management, the (s,S) policy — order up to level S when inventory falls to s — was derived by Herbert Scarf in 1960 using dynamic programming, and remains the foundation of modern supply chain optimisation. In portfolio optimisation, Robert Merton's 1971 consumption-portfolio model is solved via the HJB equation, and its discrete-time variants underpin quantitative finance. In energy systems, SDDP-based models plan hydroelectric reservoir operations across South America, and approximate DP methods optimise the dispatch of wind, solar, and battery storage under uncertain generation. In healthcare, MDPs model treatment sequencing for chronic diseases — when to escalate therapy, when to switch drugs, when to intervene surgically — with the value function encoding the patient's expected quality-adjusted life years. In autonomous vehicle control, the partially observable MDP (POMDP) framework extends the standard MDP to handle uncertain sensor readings and occluded objects. POMDPs maintain a belief state — a probability distribution over possible states — and optimise over this belief space. The computational cost is even more severe than for fully observable MDPs (the belief space is continuous even when the underlying state space is finite), but point-based value iteration methods developed by Joelle Pineau, Geoffrey Gordon, and Sebastian Thrun in the early 2000s made practical POMDP solving feasible for robotics applications. Today, POMDP-based planners operate in surgical robots, UAV mission planning, and spoken dialogue systems. Model-Free vs. Model-Based: The Central Tension The deepest conceptual divide in the field runs between model-based and model-free methods. Model-based methods — classical dynamic programming, SDDP, model predictive control — require an explicit transition model P(s'|s,a) and use it to compute or approximate the value function. Model-free methods — Q-learning, SARSA, policy gradient algorithms — learn directly from experience without ever constructing a model. Model-free methods are more flexible (they can be applied when the environment is unknown or too complex to model), but they are less sample-efficient (they require many more interactions to converge). The tension is not a clean dichotomy; modern methods like Dyna, MuZero, and world-model approaches combine both strategies, using a learned model to generate simulated experience that supplements real data. Bertsekas has argued that model predictive control and rollout algorithms — which use a base policy and a lookahead horizon — provide a principled middle ground that captures many of the benefits of both approaches. The open frontier is vast. Partial observability remains computationally daunting: exact POMDP solutions are PSPACE-complete, and scalable approximations are an active area of research. Multi-agent settings — where multiple decision-makers interact strategically — transform the Bellman equation into a system of coupled equations (the Nash-Bellman equations of stochastic games), and finding equilibria is generally intractable. Continuous state-action spaces require either discretisation (introducing approximation error) or function approximation (introducing instability — the "deadly triad" of bootstrapping, function approximation, and off-policy learning that Sutton identified as a fundamental challenge). Real-time decision-making at scale — controlling a power grid, managing a fleet of autonomous vehicles, or operating a hospital in real time — demands solution times measured in milliseconds, not hours, pushing toward online planning methods like Monte Carlo tree search and real-time dynamic programming. Distributionally robust dynamic programming, which optimises against the worst-case distribution within an ambiguity set rather than a single assumed model, has emerged as a principled approach to model uncertainty — the recognition that the transition probabilities themselves are never known exactly. Offline reinforcement learning, which learns policies from pre-collected datasets without further interaction, addresses the safety constraints that prevent online exploration in healthcare, autonomous driving, and financial trading. And the integration of large language models as world models — using their implicit knowledge of physical and social dynamics to simulate consequences of actions — represents the newest, most speculative frontier. Classification: Mathematical optimisation framework for sequential decision-making under uncertainty; foundation of Markov decision processes, optimal control, and reinforcement learning. FACTUAL INTEGRITY DISCLOSURE

docs.title: The Bridge Between Time Series and Tabular ML: A Systematic Guide to Feature Engineering
docs.subtitle: Lags, rolling statistics, Fourier transforms, calendar features, and the leakage traps that invalidate everything.
docs.body: 1. Why Feature Engineering Is the Forecast Your LightGBM model doesn't know it's forecasting a time series. It sees rows and columns — a tabular dataset like any other. The temporal intelligence — trend, seasonality, autocorrelation, external drivers — lives entirely in the features you engineer. If you create good lag features, the model learns from recent history. If you create good rolling statistics, it captures trend and volatility. If you create good calendar features, it captures seasonality. If you create bad features, or leak future information into them, no model and no hyperparameter tuning can save you. This is the article that the Shapash time series guide assumed you'd already read. Every SHAP explanation, every Shapash dashboard, every EBM shape function explains features, not the underlying time series. "lag1 contributed +$18k" is only meaningful if lag1 was correctly engineered — shifted to prevent leakage, named for human readability, and grouped with related lags for stakeholder presentation. Feature engineering quality determines both forecast accuracy and explanation quality. This article covers every feature family systematically: lags, rolling statistics, calendar, Fourier, differencing, exogenous regressors, and interactions. It covers the leakage traps that invalidate pipelines. It covers automated extraction with tsfresh and featuretools. And it covers the connection to explainability — why your feature choices determine what SHAP and Shapash can tell you. Every concept section contains a runnable notebook cell. 💡 Notebook Map Cells 0–9: Cell 0 Setup (§1) · Cell 1 Lags (§3) · Cell 2 Rolling Stats (§4) · Cell 3 Calendar + Fourier (§5–6) · Cell 4 Exogenous + Diff (§7–8) · Cell 5 Interaction Features (§9) · Cell 6 Train Model (§11) · Cell 7 PFI Feature Selection (§12) · Cell 8 Leakage Audit (§10) · Cell 9 Explainability Labels (§13) 2. The Core Transformation The fundamental idea: each row in the tabular dataset represents one forecast origin — the point in time from which you make a prediction. Each column represents one engineered feature that captures temporal information available at that origin. The target column is the value you're predicting (demand at time t+h, where h is the forecast horizon). This framing converts any time series problem into a supervised regression problem that any tabular model can solve. Raw time series (left) becomes a tabular dataset (right) where each row is a forecast origin and each column is an engineered feature. The model sees only rows and columns — the temporal intelligence lives entirely in the features. The forecast origin concept is critical for avoiding leakage. At each origin time t, you can only use information available up to time t (for a next-step forecast h=1) or up to time t−h+1 (for a direct multi-step forecast of horizon h). Any feature that incorporates information from time t+1 or later is leaking future data. This constraint determines which lags, rolling windows, and exogenous variables you can use. For direct multi-step forecasting (predicting h steps ahead in one shot), the minimum usable lag is h itself. You cannot use lag1 for a 7-day-ahead forecast because yesterday's value won't be known when you make the forecast 7 days in advance. This is the most common mistake in multi-step forecasting pipelines. The forecast horizon constrains which lags are available. For h=1, all lags work. For h=7, only lag7 and beyond are valid — lag1 through lag6 are not yet observed at prediction time. 3. Lag Features: Carrying History Lag features are the most important feature family for time series ML. A lag feature lagk is simply the target value k time steps ago: lagk(t) = y(t−k). Lag1 carries yesterday's demand, lag7 carries same-day-last-week, lag365 carries same-day-last-year. Together, lags encode the autocorrelation structure — the tendency of a time series to be similar to its recent past. Which lags to include? The ACF (Autocorrelation Function) and PACF (Partial Autocorrelation Function) tell you. Significant ACF spikes at lags 1, 7, and 14 mean the series has daily autocorrelation, weekly seasonality, and biweekly patterns. The PACF shows direct dependencies (controlling for intermediate lags) — use PACF for parsimonious lag selection. In practice, a safe default for daily data: lags 1, 2, 3, 7, 14, 21, 28, 365. For hourly: add lags 24, 48, 168 (one week). ACF shows total correlation; PACF shows direct dependencies. Use PACF spikes to select which lags to engineer. 4. Rolling Window Statistics: Trend and Volatility Rolling statistics aggregate recent history into summary measures: the 7-day rolling mean captures short-term trend, the 28-day rolling mean captures medium-term trend, and the rolling standard deviation captures volatility. Together, they tell the model: "where has the series been recently, how fast is it changing, and how noisy is it?"' The critical leakage rule: rolling statistics must be computed on df["value"].shift(1), not df["value"]. If you compute df["value"].rolling(7).mean(), the window at time t includes the value at time t — which is the target you're trying to predict. Shifting by 1 before rolling ensures the window only includes past values. This is the #1 bug in time series ML pipelines, and it inflates accuracy by 5-15% while producing misleading explanations. Beyond mean and std, consider: rolling min (recent floor), rolling max (recent ceiling), rolling median (robust central tendency), rolling skew (asymmetry), and rolling quantiles (distributional shape). The coefficient of variation (rollingstd / rollingmean) is an underrated feature that captures whether volatility is proportional to level — important for multiplicative seasonality. ⚠️ The #1 leakage trap Always compute rolling stats on df[target].shift(1), not df[target]. Without the shift, the rolling window at time t includes the value at time t — the target you're predicting. This inflates accuracy by 5-15% and produces misleading SHAP explanations. Every rolling, expanding, and EWM feature must be shifted. 5. Calendar Features: Encoding Seasonality Calendar features capture deterministic seasonality — patterns that repeat on a known schedule. Day of week (0=Monday, 6=Sunday) captures weekly cycles (weekend vs. weekday). Month (1–12) captures annual seasonality. Hour of day captures intraday patterns. Holiday flags capture irregular but predictable events. Encoding matters. Raw integers (month=1,2,...,12) imply an ordering that may not exist — is December "close to" January? For tree models, raw integers work because trees can split anywhere. For linear models or neural networks, cyclical encoding (sin/cos pairs) preserves the circular nature: sin(2π·month/12) and cos(2π·month/12) make December adjacent to January. One-hot encoding is safe but creates many columns for high-cardinality features (52 weeks, 365 days). Holiday detection uses the holidays library (country-specific calendars) or workalendar (global coverage). Beyond binary holiday flags, consider: daysuntilnextholiday, dayssincelastholiday, isbridgeday (weekday between a holiday and a weekend), and holidaytype (public, school, religious) if the data supports it. 6. Fourier Features: Smooth Periodicity Fourier features model periodic patterns as sums of sine and cosine waves at different frequencies. For a period P and harmonic k: sin(2π·k·t/P) and cos(2π·k·t/P). Each sin/cos pair captures one frequency component of the seasonality. More harmonics capture sharper patterns; fewer harmonics capture smoother patterns. Fourier features have two advantages over raw calendar features. First, they're continuous — no discontinuity between December 31 and January 1. Second, they can model smooth seasonality with fewer features than one-hot encoding (6 Fourier pairs vs. 12 month dummies). The tradeoff: Fourier features assume smooth, repeating patterns and can't capture sharp holiday effects. Use both: Fourier for smooth annual/weekly seasonality, plus explicit holiday flags for irregular events. How many harmonics? For annual seasonality with daily data: k=1,2,3 (3 pairs = 6 features) is usually sufficient. k=1 captures a single peak/trough; k=2 adds a second harmonic for asymmetric patterns; k=3 adds fine detail. More than 5 harmonics risks overfitting. Prophet uses this approach with built-in Fourier order selection. 7. Exogenous Regressors Exogenous features are external variables that influence the target but aren't derived from it — temperature for energy demand, promotion flags for retail sales, economic indicators for financial series. The key question for each exogenous variable: is it known in advance at prediction time? Known-in-advance variables (scheduled promotions, holidays, calendar events) can be used at their concurrent value — you know next Tuesday is a holiday when forecasting next Tuesday's demand. Unknown-at-prediction-time variables (temperature, competitor pricing) must be lagged — you don't know next Tuesday's temperature, so you use today's or yesterday's temperature as a feature. Using concurrent values of unknown-at-prediction-time variables is future leakage. 8. Differencing: Stationarity Transforms First differences (value(t) − value(t−1)) remove linear trend. Seasonal differences (value(t) − value(t−7)) remove weekly seasonality. Log returns (log(value(t)/value(t−1))) normalise for level-dependent volatility. These transformations make non-stationary series more amenable to tabular models — though tree-based models handle non-stationarity better than linear models, differencing features still improve accuracy by making the signal more accessible. A practical question: should you difference the target (predict the change, then cumulate) or add differencing as features (predict the level, informed by change rates)? Both work. Differencing the target can improve stationarity but requires careful inverse-transformation at prediction time. Adding diff features is simpler and works well with tree models that can naturally handle level + change information together. 9. Interaction Features Interaction features capture conditional effects — where one feature's influence depends on another's value. lag1 × isweekend captures "yesterday's demand matters differently on weekdays vs. weekends." temperature × month captures "temperature's effect on demand depends on season" (cold weather increases heating demand in winter but not in summer). Tree models discover interactions automatically via multi-way splits. But explicitly engineering key interactions can improve convergence speed and accuracy — the model doesn't need to discover the pattern through splits, it's handed the combined signal directly. For linear models and EBMs (which are additive), explicit interactions are essential — without them, conditional effects are invisible. Seven feature families, each capturing a different temporal pattern. Together they give any tabular model full forecasting capability. 10. The Leakage Problem Three leakage types. Target leakage (forgot to shift), future leakage (used concurrent exogenous), and split leakage (random train/test split). Each invalidates the pipeline. Target leakage is the most common: computing df["value"].rolling(7).mean() includes the value at time t in the rolling window for time t's prediction. The fix: always shift before rolling. Future leakage through exogenous variables: using today's temperature to predict today's demand, when in production you won't have today's temperature at forecast time. The fix: lag all unknown-at-prediction-time exogenous variables. Split leakage: random train/test split (shuffle=True) mixes past and future, letting the model "peek" at future patterns during training. The fix: always use temporal splits (shuffle=False, or explicit date-based cutoffs). How to detect leakage post-hoc: (1) Implausibly high accuracy — if your demand forecast achieves R²=0.99, something is wrong. (2) Permutation Feature Importance showing a feature as dramatically more important than domain knowledge suggests. (3) A large gap between train accuracy and cross-validated accuracy. (4) Walk-forward validation showing much lower accuracy than hold-out validation — the hold-out may have leaked. 11. Training the Model With features engineered, the training step is standard tabular ML. The critical requirement: temporal train/test split. Never shuffle time series data. Use the first 80% chronologically for training and the last 20% for testing. For hyperparameter tuning, use walk-forward cross-validation (TimeSeriesSplit in scikit-learn) — not random k-fold. LightGBM, XGBoost, and CatBoost are the typical model choices. LightGBM is fastest; CatBoost handles categoricals natively; XGBoost has the largest community. EBMs provide built-in interpretability without needing SHAP. The features we've engineered work with all of these — the tabular format is model-agnostic. 12. Feature Selection with PFI After engineering 40+ features, some will be redundant or noisy. Permutation Feature Importance (PFI) on the test set identifies features that don't contribute to generalisation — remove them to simplify the model and potentially improve accuracy. The workflow: compute PFI, remove features with importance indistinguishable from zero, retrain, re-evaluate. For time series features specifically, watch for: (1) correlated lags splitting importance (lag1 and lag2 each show low PFI because they carry similar information — but the pair is critical). (2) Fourier features showing zero importance on a dataset without smooth seasonality — remove them. (3) Interaction features showing zero importance — the tree model was already capturing the interaction via splits. 13. The Explainability Connection Every XAI tool explains features, not the underlying time series. Feature quality and naming determine explanation quality. SHAP says "lag1 contributed +$18,200 to this forecast." Shapash translates this to "Yesterday's Value: $210k → +$18,200." An EBM's shape function shows exactly how f(lag1) maps yesterday's value to today's prediction contribution. All three are explaining features. If the feature is poorly named (feature23 instead of "Yesterday's Value"), the explanation is useless. If the feature leaks (unshifted rolling mean), the explanation is wrong. If important features are ungrouped (43 individual SHAP bars), the explanation is overwhelming. The featuresdict pattern from Shapash: map every engineered column to a human-readable label. The featuresgroups pattern: collapse related features into business-meaningful groups ("Recent History," "Trend," "Seasonality," "Weather"). These are not post-hoc cosmetics — they're design decisions that determine whether the explanation is actionable for non-technical stakeholders. ✅ End of notebook Cells 0–9 complete: synthetic data → lags → rolling stats → calendar + Fourier → exogenous + differencing → interactions → model training → PFI selection → leakage audit → explainability labels. Copy green-bordered cells in order for a working pipeline. 14. Automated Feature Engineering Four automated FE libraries. tsfresh: comprehensive, 750+ features. featuretools: relational data. tsfel: lightweight. MLforecast: Nixtla ecosystem. tsfresh (Christ et al., Neurocomputing 2018) automatically extracts 750+ statistical features — mean, variance, autocorrelation at multiple lags, spectral entropy, number of peaks, benford correlation, and hundreds more. It then applies hypothesis testing (Benjamini-Hochberg correction) to filter irrelevant features. Strength: discovery of unexpected patterns. Weakness: massive feature explosion (thousands of features before filtering), computational cost, and loss of interpretability — "agglineartrendattrstderrchunklen50" is not a feature your VP can understand. featuretools uses Deep Feature Synthesis to automatically generate features from relational datasets (e.g., transactions × customers × products). Better for transactional/relational time series than pure time series. tsfel is a lightweight alternative to tsfresh with fewer but well-curated features. MLforecast (Nixtla) provides lag transforms and rolling features integrated with the Nixtla forecasting ecosystem. After automated extraction, feature selection is mandatory. Use PFI, Boruta, or RFECV to reduce tsfresh's 750+ features to a manageable set. The recommended workflow: (1) automated extraction for discovery, (2) PFI-based selection to keep only useful features, (3) manual review to verify interpretability and name features for explanation. 15. Use Case: Retail Demand Forecasting Daily/weekly, cost-optimisation. A retail chain forecasts per-store daily demand using 35 features: lags (1, 2, 3, 7, 14, 28), rolling stats (7d, 14d, 28d mean and std), calendar (dow, month, isweekend, isholiday), Fourier (weekly + annual, 3 harmonics each), exogenous (promotion flag, temperature lag1), and interactions (lag1 × isweekend, promotion × month). LightGBM achieves MAPE 3.2%. The explainability payoff: Shapash shows the regional manager: "Today's forecast is $182k. Main drivers: Yesterday's Sales: $175k (+$12k), Weekend: Yes (+$8k), Active Promotion (+$6k), 7-Day Average: trending up (+$4k)." The grouping collapses 35 features into 6 business-meaningful groups. Without feature engineering, there's nothing to explain. The leakage lesson: An early version of the pipeline used unshifted rolling7dmean and achieved MAPE 1.1% — suspiciously good. PFI showed rolling7dmean as 5x more important than any other feature. Investigation revealed the rolling window included the target day. After fixing with .shift(1), MAPE rose to 3.2% — the real performance. The 1.1% was an illusion. 16. Use Case: Energy Load Forecasting Hourly, real-time, safety-critical. A utility forecasts hourly electricity demand using 52 features: lags (1h, 2h, 3h, 24h, 168h), rolling stats (6h, 12h, 24h, 168h), calendar (hour, dow, month, holiday), Fourier (daily + weekly + annual), weather (temperature, humidity, wind — all lagged by 1–3 hours), and interactions (temp × hour, holiday × hour). Gradient boosting achieves MAE 1.2 MW. The calendar feature insight: Hourof_day captures the intraday load curve (peaks at 8–9am and 6–8pm). But the interaction temp × hour captures that cold weather increases evening demand more than morning demand — a conditional effect that calendar features alone miss. This interaction feature improved MAE by 3% on winter evenings specifically. Feature engineering for production: Features must be computable in real-time with only past data available. Temperature lags require a weather data pipeline with at most 1-hour delay. Rolling stats must use the exact same shift logic as training. Any discrepancy between training and serving feature computation invalidates the model. 17. Manual vs. Automated vs. Deep Learning Three approaches: manual FE + tabular ML (this article), built-in components (Prophet), and end-to-end deep learning (TFT, N-BEATS). Each has strengths. Manual feature engineering + tabular model (this article's approach) gives full control, interpretability, and domain knowledge injection. The features are visible, nameable, and explainable. The model is fast to train and cheap to serve. It requires engineering expertise and time. Prophet / NeuralProphet builds in trend, seasonality, holidays, and regressors as model components — faster to start, less control, adequate for many forecasting tasks. Deep learning (N-BEATS, TFT, PatchTST, Chronos) learns features end-to-end from raw sequences — no manual engineering, but requires more data, more compute, and produces less explainable models. The empirical evidence: on structured business forecasting problems (demand, energy, sales), manual FE + gradient boosting matches or beats deep learning in most benchmarks (Makridakis M5, Kaggle time series competitions). Deep learning wins when the data is abundant, the patterns are complex, and interpretability is not required. The choice depends on your constraints: if you need explanations, manual FE + tabular. If you need automation at scale across thousands of series, Prophet or deep learning. 18. Decision Framework ✓ Use manual feature engineering when: You need interpretable forecasts (SHAP, Shapash, EBM shape functions). You have domain knowledge to encode (known seasonality, known external drivers). The forecast will be explained to non-technical stakeholders. You're forecasting few series with deep history (retail store, energy grid). Accuracy on structured business data needs to be competitive with deep learning. Production latency requires fast inference (tabular models serve in milliseconds). ✗ Consider alternatives when: You have thousands of diverse series to forecast → Prophet, Chronos, or global DL models. You lack domain expertise for feature design → automated FE (tsfresh) or deep learning. The patterns are too complex for hand-crafted features → TFT, PatchTST. Interpretability is not required → end-to-end DL may be simpler. You need uncertainty quantification → probabilistic DL (DeepAR) or conformal prediction. Feature engineering is the bridge between time series and tabular ML. It is also the bridge between time series and explainability. Every SHAP value, every Shapash dashboard, every EBM shape function explains the features you engineered. Build them well — with correct shifting, meaningful naming, and thoughtful grouping — and the entire downstream stack works. Build them poorly, and no model, no explanation tool, and no dashboard can compensate. Sources: Hyndman & Athanasopoulos, "Forecasting: Principles and Practice" 3rd ed. (OTexts); Box & Jenkins 1970; Christ, Braun, Neuffer, Kempa-Liehr, Neurocomputing 2018 (tsfresh); scikit-learn TimeSeriesSplit docs; LightGBM docs; Shapash docs (MAIF). Practice: Makridakis M5 competition; Kaggle time series competitions; dotdata feature engineering guide; Nixtla MLforecast. Software: pandas, NumPy, statsmodels (ACF/PACF), tsfresh, featuretools, tsfel, MLforecast, holidays library. pandas · NumPy · scikit-learn · LightGBM · tsfresh Length determined by information, not word count /div> /div>);}

docs.title: Splitwise in Python: The Complete Practitioner's Guide
docs.subtitle: From OAuth handshakes to debt-minimisation graphs — everything a data engineer needs to wire Python into Splitwise's REST API.
docs.body: 01. What is Splitwise? — s1 02. The Python SDK Landscape — s2 03. Authentication Deep Dive — s3 04. Core Object Model — s4 05. Reading Data — s5 06. Writing Data — s6 07. Analytics & Reporting — s7 08. Rate Limits & Best Practices — s8 09. Real-World Use Cases — s9 10. API Reference Cheatsheet — s10 Splitwise in Python: The Complete Practitioner's Guide From OAuth handshakes to debt-minimisation graphs — everything a data engineer, automation developer, or financial-analysis practitioner needs to wire Python directly into Splitwise's REST API using the splitwise SDK. What Is Splitwise? Splitwise is a debt-tracking and expense-splitting platform launched in 2011, now serving tens of millions of users globally. Unlike a payment processor, it is a ledger of obligations — it records who paid what, computes who owes whom, and optimises the resulting debt graph to minimise the number of settlement transactions required. At its core, Splitwise solves a surprisingly hard combinatorial problem: given N people with M expenses between them, find the minimum set of payments that resolves every debt. Naively, this is O(N²) bilateral settlements; Splitwise's "Simplify Debts" feature reduces this to at most N-1 transfers by treating the problem as a flow network. The platform exposes a full REST API at https://secure.splitwise.com/api/v3.0/, supporting OAuth 1.0a, OAuth 2.0, and personal API keys. Every resource — users, groups, expenses, comments, currencies, categories — is accessible programmatically. This makes Splitwise an attractive target for financial automation, household budget analysis, and shared-economy tooling. 💡 Key Concept Expenses not assigned to a group are placed in a virtual group with id=0. Always filter getExpenses() results by getPayment() == False to exclude settlement transactions from spending analyses. The Python SDK Landscape Several community SDKs exist across languages for the Splitwise API. For Python, the canonical choice is namaggarwal/splitwise, a 208-star MIT-licensed package on PyPI that provides complete typed object wrappers around every API endpoint. It is the only Python SDK listed on the official Splitwise developer documentation. The SDK supports Python 3.7 through 3.11 and runs on PyPy. It wraps every API resource as a Python class with explicit getter and setter methods — a deliberate design choice that provides IDE autocomplete at the cost of more verbose code than a simple dict-based client. Authentication Deep Dive Splitwise supports three authentication mechanisms. Choosing the wrong one for your use case is the most common source of integration friction. Understanding the tradeoffs upfront saves hours of debugging. For personal automation — monthly spending reports, household budget dashboards, CLI tools — the API key is the fastest path. Register an application at https://secure.splitwise.com/oauthclients, then use the API key you receive directly. This method acts on your account only and is not suitable for multi-user applications. 💡 Security Note Never commit API keys to version control. Store them in a .env file loaded via python-dotenv, or use environment variables in your CI/CD pipeline. The .env file should be in .gitignore. For multi-user applications, OAuth 2.0 is mandatory. The SDK handles the full authorization code flow. The critical security step is validating the state parameter on callback to prevent CSRF attacks — the SDK returns it from getOAuth2AuthorizeURL() and you must compare it to what comes back from Splitwise. "Store the access token after the first OAuth exchange — Splitwise tokens do not expire, making them safe to persist in your database for long-running automations." splitwise SDK documentation Core Object Model Every API response is deserialised into a typed Python object. Understanding the object hierarchy is essential for writing efficient, non-redundant code. The central entry point is the Splitwise class; all objects it returns are instances of the following classes from the splitwise package. 💡 Gotcha getCost() returns a string, not a float. Always cast: float(expense.getCost()). Similarly, getOwedShare() and getPaidShare() on ExpenseUser objects are also strings. Reading Data The getExpenses() method is the workhorse of any Splitwise integration. It supports rich server-side filtering, but has a hard limit of 100 results per call, requiring pagination for any account with significant history. 💡 Filter Parameters datedafter / datedbefore — ISO 8601 strings updatedafter — for incremental sync groupid — filter by group friendid — bilateral expenses limit — max 100 per page offset — pagination cursor The pattern above — paginating until an empty page is returned, then converting to a pandas DataFrame — is the recommended foundation for any analytical pipeline. Filter getDeletedAt() immediately; deleted expenses are returned by default and will corrupt aggregations if not excluded. For incremental pipelines, use updatedafter instead of datedafter. This captures edits and deletions to previously synced expenses, which datedafter misses entirely. Writing Data Creating expenses programmatically requires careful attention to the paidshare and owedshare fields on each ExpenseUser. These are the most common source of API errors for new integrators: the sum of all paidshare values must equal the total expense cost, and the sum of all owedshare values must also equal the total cost. 💡 Invariant Always verify: sum(paidshares) == totalcost and sum(owedshares) == total_cost. Violating either will return a validation error from the API. Float precision errors are a common cause — use round(value, 2) and adjust the last share to absorb rounding. updateExpense() accepts a partial Expense object with only the fields to change — you do not need to re-specify the full expense. The most ergonomic pattern is to first getExpense(id), mutate the fetched object, then pass it to updateExpense(). Deletion via deleteExpense(id) is a soft delete — the expense is retained in API responses with a non-null getDeletedAt(). Analytics & Reporting This is where the Python SDK truly differentiates itself from Splitwise's native app. By combining the SDK with pandas, you can build financial dashboards, categorised spending reports, and group fairness analyses that the app simply does not offer. The monthly pivot table pattern is particularly useful for household financial reviews. The key insight is filtering getPayment() == True to exclude settlement transfers, which are not actual expenditures. Without this filter, a £200 Venmo repayment from a flatmate inflates your apparent spending by that amount. Rate Limits & Best Practices Splitwise's API does not publish formal rate limit documentation, but community experience places the practical ceiling at approximately 60 requests per minute. Scripts that iterate over large expense histories using per-expense getExpense(id) calls will hit this limit quickly — always prefer bulk operations like getExpenses() with filters over individual fetches. Real-World Use Cases The following use cases represent the most common and highest-value applications practitioners build on the Splitwise Python SDK. Each maps a concrete problem to the specific SDK methods required. API Reference Cheatsheet A quick-reference index of every primary SDK method. All methods are called on a Splitwise instance (sw) after authentication. 💡 Further Reading Official Splitwise REST API docs: https://dev.splitwise.com/ SDK source + examples: https://github.com/namaggarwal/splitwise Flask example app: https://github.com/namaggarwal/flask-splitwise-example SDK readthedocs: https://splitwise.readthedocs.io/

docs.title: SAFE: Surrogate-Assisted Feature Extraction for Time Series
docs.subtitle: Extracting monotonic, piecewise-constant feature transformations from black-box time series models using ALE profiles and surrogate trees.
docs.body: SAFE: Surrogate-Assisted Feature Extraction How the MI²DataLab method bridges the black-box performance gap for interpretable time series forecasting — and how it compares to seven modern alternatives. 01. The Problem in Time Series Time series forecasting sits at an uncomfortable junction. On one side stands the practitioner deploying an XGBoost model with 200 lag features, a calendar effect matrix, and rolling statistics — achieving 6% MASE on the M4 benchmark but unable to explain to the energy regulator why the model predicts a demand spike at 18:00 on a Wednesday in January. On the other side stands the analyst building a linear regression with hand-crafted features: explainable at every coefficient, but routinely 15–25% worse in forecast accuracy. This is not a new dilemma, but it sharpens in time series for three structural reasons. First, the feature space is inherently high-dimensional: a 30-day lookback window on a daily series with 10 exogenous variables immediately produces 300+ candidate features, and their pairwise interactions — lag₁ × temperature, lag₇ × holidayflag — multiply this combinatorially. Second, the features are autocorrelated by construction: lag₁, lag₂, lag₃ all carry overlapping information, which breaks the independence assumption underpinning Partial Dependence Plots. Third, the data-generating process is non-stationary: a threshold relationship estimated on 2022 data may not hold in 2024 after a regime shift. SAFE's answer to this dilemma is to treat the black-box not as a competitor but as a feature engineer. The black-box learns which regions of the lag and exogenous variable space behave differently. SAFE extracts those regional boundaries — the breakpoints — and re-encodes them as explicit, named indicator features that a linear model can use as coefficients, a regulator can audit, and a domain expert can recognise as meaningful. The canonical paper — Gosiewska, Kozak & Biecek (2021), "Simpler is Better: Lifting the Interpretability-Performance Trade-off via Automated Feature Engineering", published in Decision Support Systems — benchmarks SAFE across 30 tabular datasets and finds that a logistic or linear regression trained on SAFE-transformed features closes 60–85% of the performance gap between a baseline linear model and a black-box, while retaining full coefficient interpretability. The rSAFE R package (Gosiewska, Gierlak, Biecek; CRAN v0.1.4, last updated 2022-08-13) provides the canonical implementation. A Python equivalent, SafeTransformer, mirrors the sklearn API. 02. SAFE Algorithm — Canonical 03rSAFE: The R Reference Implementation The rSAFE package was developed at the MI²DataLab (Warsaw University of Technology) as part of the broader DrWhy.AI ecosystem for responsible machine learning. The canonical demo uses the apartments dataset from DALEX — predicting price per m² from construction year, surface, floor, number of rooms, and district. We annotate this demo fully, as every parameter choice has a direct analogue in the time series context. AIC: most breakpoints, best for exploratory analysis. BIC/MBIC: conservative — prefer these for production; MBIC is the rSAFE default and works well for time series lag features with moderate autocorrelation. Numeric value (e.g. 25): manual override — use when you have domain knowledge that a specific number of regimes is expected (e.g. a known 3-regime tariff structure in energy data). 04. Adapting SAFE to Time Series Step 0: Supervised Embedding (Lag Matrix Construction) SAFE operates on tabular feature matrices. Time series must first be transformed into a supervised regression format via a sliding window embedding. Given a univariate series y₁, y₂, ..., yₙ with lookback window w, each row of the lag matrix is [yₜ₋ᵥ, ..., yₜ₋₁, exog1ₜ, ..., exogkₜ] → target yₜ. Exogenous features (temperature, calendar dummies, rolling means) are appended column-wise. This is standard practice — the SAFE pipeline then treats this matrix exactly as any tabular dataset. Always set responsetype = "ale" for time series lag features. Lag features are structurally correlated: lag₁ and lag₂ share ~90% of their variance on a typical demand series. PDP computes its marginal effect by averaging predictions across the empirical distribution of all other features — but for correlated lags this means evaluating the model at (lag₁=100, lag₂=5), a combination that never occurs in reality. ALE restricts averaging to a narrow neighbourhood of the actual feature value, eliminating this extrapolation artefact. The rSAFE documentation explicitly states: "if features are uncorrelated, one can use 'pdp' — otherwise 'ale' is strongly recommended." Lag features are never uncorrelated. safelydetectchangepoints() for Structural Break Detection This rSAFE function applies penalised likelihood changepoint detection (using the changepoint package internally) to the ALE/PDP profile of each feature. In a time series context, the extracted breakpoints often correspond to regime thresholds with direct domain interpretability: the lag-1 value above which demand enters a peak-consumption regime; the rolling-7-day mean temperature above which HVAC load saturates; the day-of-year at which holiday effects activate. These are features a domain expert would want to hand-engineer but would need weeks to identify empirically. safelydetectinteractions() for Lag × Exogenous Discovery The safelydetectinteractions() function computes the H-statistic (Friedman & Popescu) for each pair of features in the black-box's prediction surface. In time series, the most diagnostically valuable interactions are between lag features and exogenous variables: does the effect of lag₁ on demand differ depending on whether it is a working day? Does the temperature effect vary by hour of day? SAFE surfaces these as interaction features, expressed as products of indicator columns, which a GLM can then represent as a coefficient on the interaction term. Never extract SAFE breakpoints on the full dataset before cross-validation. Breakpoints are estimated from the black-box's predictions on training data. If you fit the black-box on all data, then extract breakpoints, then cross-validate the white-box — you have leaked future information into the feature engineering step. Always use TimeSeriesSplit (Python) or rsample::timeseriessplit() (R), and call safeextraction() inside the training fold only. 05. Mathematical Foundations Partial Dependence vs Accumulated Local Effects Changepoint Detection Objective Surrogate Fidelity SAFE does not require an explicit surrogate model — the black-box itself is used directly through the DALEX explainer. However, a critical quality gate is the R² fidelity of the ALE profiles in capturing the black-box's behaviour. If the ALE curve for a feature is flat (low variance), no meaningful breakpoints exist for that feature — safeextraction() will correctly return no intervals. A flat ALE is informative: it means the black-box treats that feature as unimportant or linear, and the original numeric form should be preferred. Interaction Measure 06. Python Implementation The Python SafeTransformer mirrors the rSAFE pipeline with a scikit-learn compatible API. The implementation below extends it specifically for time series, adding lag embedding, TimeSeriesSplit guard-railing, and ALE computation via the alibi library. 07. Worked Forecasting Benchmark The following benchmark uses the ETT (Electricity Transformer Temperature) dataset — a standard M4/ETT benchmark used across NeurIPS 2022 time series papers. We predict 1-step-ahead transformer oil temperature (OT) using 7 lag features and 2 engineered calendar features, comparing three models: baseline Ridge, XGBoost black-box ceiling, and SAFE-enhanced Ridge. 08. Competitive Landscape SAFE occupies a specific niche between automated feature engineering and explainability-guided model building. Seven alternatives address overlapping concerns from different angles. FI-SHAP is the closest direct competitor — if you are committed to a boosting model and want SHAP-guided feature construction, it is more tightly integrated with the model's gradient structure than SAFE. tsfresh wins when you have no domain knowledge and want a comprehensive feature library for classification tasks. GAMs/EBMs remove the two-stage pipeline entirely — if the primary goal is interpretability and you accept model-specific shape functions rather than GLM coefficients, EBMs are architecturally cleaner. SAFE wins when you must produce a standard GLM with interval coefficients auditable by a regulator, but want the black-box's discovered structure rather than hand-crafted domain features. Method Deep-Dives 09. Limitations Specific to Time Series 1. Data Leakage via Temporal Ordering The most dangerous failure mode. If safeextraction() is called on the full dataset before splitting into train/test folds, the breakpoints are contaminated by future observations. This is not hypothetical — it is easy to do accidentally when using rSAFE on a pre-split dataset where the explainer was built on test-period data. Always call safeextraction() inside the training fold, and transform test data using an extractor fitted on training data only. 2. Autocorrelated Features Violate PDP Independence As detailed in Section 04, PDP is analytically invalid for lag features. The consequence is not just theoretical: in our ETT benchmark, using responsetype='pdp' instead of 'ale' extracts breakpoints at physically implausible lag₁ values — artifacts of the model being evaluated at (lag₁=high, lag₂=low) combinations that never appear in the data. ALE eliminates this. The rSAFE documentation is explicit on this point. 3. Non-Stationarity: Breakpoints Drift Breakpoints estimated on 2022 electricity demand data may not reflect the 2024 distribution if a heat pump adoption wave has shifted the temperature-demand relationship upward. SAFE provides no mechanism for online breakpoint updating. Practical mitigation: re-run the full SAFE pipeline periodically (monthly or quarterly), monitor whether breakpoint positions shift by more than one bin width, and trigger a re-fit if they do. This is equivalent to a feature engineering drift monitor. 4. Univariate Changepoint Detection Only safelydetectchangepoints() operates on the 1D ALE profile of each feature independently. It cannot detect multivariate regime shifts — where the joint distribution of (lag₁, temperature) changes simultaneously. For energy forecasting, the most economically important regime shifts (e.g. a cold snap that triggers both high lag values and high temperature-demand sensitivity) are inherently multivariate. SAFE's interaction detection via safelydetectinteractions() partially addresses this, but the interaction features are constructed from univariate breakpoints rather than jointly estimated. 5. No Online Learning Mechanism rSAFE v0.1.4 (the current CRAN release) has no streaming or incremental update capability. Every re-extraction requires a full black-box refit on the updated training window. For high-frequency forecasting (hourly, sub-hourly), this may be computationally prohibitive. The Python SafeTransformer similarly has no partialfit interface. Recommended production monitoring: (1) Track the distribution of each SAFE indicator feature weekly — a shift in I(lag1 > t₁) activation rate by >15% signals potential breakpoint drift. (2) Re-run safe_extraction() on a rolling 12-month window quarterly. (3) Compare new breakpoints to existing ones; if a breakpoint shifts by more than 10% of the feature range, retrain the white-box with updated features. 10. Practitioner Decision Guide Configuration Reference by Deployment Profile rSAFE Configuration Reference (R)

docs.title: RAG in 2026: Still Essential, No Longer Default
docs.subtitle: Long-context models can swallow 2 million tokens. Agents can search, verify, and reason in real time. Fine-tuning bakes knowledge into weights.
docs.body: 1. The Question Everyone Is Asking It's April 2026. Your team has spent six months building a RAG pipeline — chunking 50,000 internal documents, embedding them with OpenAI's text-embedding-3-large, storing them in Pinecone, retrieving top-5 chunks per query, reranking with Cohere Rerank, and generating answers with Claude. The pipeline works. Accuracy is 87% on your evaluation set. Latency is 2.3 seconds. Cost is $0.004 per query. Then your CTO reads an article about Gemini 2.0 with its 2-million-token context window, watches a demo of an AI agent that searches the web and reads documents in real time, and asks: "Why are we maintaining all this infrastructure when the model can just read everything?" It's a fair question. In 2023, when context windows were 8K tokens and models hallucinated constantly, RAG was the only viable architecture for knowledge-grounded AI. In 2026, four paradigm shifts — long-context models, agentic AI, fine-tuning advances, and graph-based retrieval — have fundamentally changed the calculus. RAG is not dead. But it is no longer the default answer to every knowledge-grounding problem. The decision is now: retrieval vs. context stuffing vs. fine-tuning vs. agentic tool-use — and the answer depends on scale, freshness, cost, auditability, and accuracy requirements. This article covers everything: the original RAG formulation and why it was a breakthrough; the paradigm shifts that challenge its relevance; where RAG remains essential and where it's being replaced; the competitive landscape of frameworks and managed services; advanced patterns from Self-RAG to Agentic RAG; evaluation methodology; and an honest decision framework for 2026. Every concept section contains a runnable notebook cell — green-bordered cells 0–9 form a complete working RAG pipeline. 💡 Notebook Map Cells 0–9 woven throughout: Cell 0 Setup (§1) · Cell 1 Chunking (§3) · Cell 2 Embedding + Vector Store (§4) · Cell 3 Retrieval + Reranking (§5) · Cell 4 RAG Chain (§6) · Cell 5 Self-RAG Pattern (§9) · Cell 6 Agentic RAG (§10) · Cell 7 Evaluation (§14) · Cell 8 Long Context Comparison (§7) · Cell 9 Decision Router (§17) The classic RAG pipeline: query → embed → retrieve → rerank → augment prompt → generate → cite. Each stage maps to a notebook cell in this article. 2. The Original RAG: Lewis et al. (NeurIPS 2020) Retrieval-Augmented Generation was formalised by Patrick Lewis, Ethan Perez, and colleagues at Meta AI (FAIR) in their NeurIPS 2020 paper. The core idea: instead of relying solely on the parametric knowledge stored in a language model's weights, augment the generation process with non-parametric memory — a retrieval step that fetches relevant documents from an external corpus. The probabilistic formulation is: p(y|x) = Σz p(y|x,z) · p(z|x), where x is the input query, z is a retrieved document, and y is the generated output. The model marginalises over retrieved documents, combining retrieval probability with generation probability. The original implementation used DPR (Dense Passage Retrieval, Karpukhin et al. 2020) as the retriever — a dual-encoder architecture that embeds queries and passages into a shared vector space, where relevance is measured by inner product. The generator was BART (Lewis et al. 2020), a sequence-to-sequence model. Two variants were proposed: RAG-Sequence (one document per generation) and RAG-Token (different documents can be retrieved for different tokens). The paper demonstrated state-of-the-art results on open-domain QA, fact verification, and knowledge-intensive NLP tasks — outperforming models with 25x more parameters. Why this was a breakthrough: it decoupled knowledge storage from knowledge use. A language model no longer needed to memorise every fact during pre-training — it could look facts up at inference time from an updatable external store. This meant knowledge could be refreshed without retraining, attributed to specific sources, and controlled by the operator. These three properties — freshness, attribution, and control — remain the core value proposition of RAG in 2026, even as the implementation details have changed entirely. 3. Chunking: The Most Important RAG Decision Before retrieval can work, documents must be split into chunks — segments small enough to be embedded and retrieved individually but large enough to carry meaningful context. This sounds like plumbing. In practice, chunking quality determines RAG quality more than the choice of embedding model, vector database, or LLM. A poorly chunked corpus produces irrelevant retrievals regardless of how good the rest of the pipeline is. Six chunking strategies, from simplest (fixed-size) to most sophisticated (late chunking). Each trades off simplicity against semantic coherence. Parent-document and recursive chunking are the current production defaults. Fixed-size chunking (512 tokens with 50-token overlap) is the simplest: it's fast, deterministic, and requires no NLP. But it breaks sentences mid-thought and splits related paragraphs into separate chunks. Sentence-level chunking respects natural language boundaries but produces uneven chunk sizes. Semantic chunking uses embedding similarity to detect topic shifts and splits at natural breakpoints — more expensive but produces coherent topical chunks. Recursive chunking (LangChain's default) splits hierarchically: first by double-newlines, then by single-newlines, then by sentences — respecting document structure. Parent-document chunking retrieves small chunks for precision but returns the larger parent document for context — the best of both worlds for many use cases. Late chunking (Jina AI, 2024) embeds the full document first, then chunks after embedding — preserving cross-chunk context that other methods lose. 4. Embeddings and Vector Search Chunks become vectors through embedding models — neural networks that map text to dense numerical representations where semantic similarity corresponds to geometric proximity. The embedding landscape in 2026 is dominated by a handful of models: OpenAI's text-embedding-3-large (3072 dimensions, best-in-class on MTEB benchmarks), Cohere's embed-v3 (1024 dimensions, multilingual), BGE (BAAI, open-source, strong on retrieval tasks), E5-mistral (Microsoft, instruction-tuned), GTE (Alibaba), and Jina's v3 embeddings (late-interaction architecture). The choice matters less than most practitioners think — the top 5 models are within 2-3% of each other on standard benchmarks. What matters more is matching the embedding model to your domain and keeping the chunking strategy consistent. Vector databases store these embeddings and support fast approximate nearest-neighbour (ANN) search. The landscape: Pinecone (fully managed, serverless, simplest to operate), Weaviate (hybrid search built-in, strong filtering), Qdrant (Rust-based, fast, good developer experience), Milvus (distributed, petabyte-scale), Chroma (lightweight, Python-native, great for prototyping), pgvector (PostgreSQL extension, use when you already have Postgres), FAISS (Meta, in-memory, fastest for pure vector search but no database features). For production enterprise RAG, Pinecone and Weaviate dominate; for prototyping and small-scale, Chroma; for existing Postgres infrastructure, pgvector. 5. Retrieval and Reranking Raw vector search returns the top-k chunks by cosine similarity — but similarity is not relevance. A chunk about "work from home policies" and a chunk about "home insurance coverage" may have similar embeddings but very different relevance to a question about remote work. This is where reranking enters: a cross-encoder model that takes the query and each candidate chunk as a pair and scores their relevance more precisely than the bi-encoder used for initial retrieval. Cross-encoders like Cohere Rerank, ColBERT (Khattab, Stanford), and BGE-reranker-v2 can improve retrieval precision by 15-30% on typical benchmarks. Hybrid search — combining BM25 (keyword/lexical) with dense (vector) retrieval — is now the production default. BM25 catches exact term matches that dense retrieval misses (acronyms, product codes, legal clause numbers); dense retrieval catches semantic matches that BM25 misses ("What's our WFH policy?" matching a document titled "Remote Work Guidelines"). The fusion is typically done via Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF): score = Σ 1/(k + ranki) across the retrieval methods. Weaviate and Elasticsearch support hybrid search natively; for other vector stores, implement it at the application layer. The key insight: retrieval precision matters more than retrieval recall for generation quality. Retrieving 10 chunks of which 3 are relevant produces better generation than retrieving 20 chunks of which 3 are relevant — because the irrelevant chunks dilute the signal and can mislead the generator. This is the "retrieved garbage in, hallucinated garbage out" problem. Reranking is the most cost-effective intervention to fix it. 6. The RAG Chain: Retrieval Meets Generation The RAG chain connects retrieval to generation: retrieved chunks are formatted into a prompt, sent to an LLM alongside the user's question, and the LLM generates an answer grounded in the retrieved context. The prompt template is critical — it must instruct the model to answer only from the provided context, cite its sources, and say "I don't know" when the context doesn't contain the answer. Without these instructions, the model will happily hallucinate an answer using its parametric knowledge, defeating the purpose of retrieval. In 2026, the RAG chain is typically orchestrated via LangChain's LCEL (LangChain Expression Language) or LlamaIndex's query engine abstraction. The key design decisions are: how many chunks to include (typically 3–7 after reranking), whether to include metadata (source document, page number, date), and how to handle multi-turn conversations (retrieve fresh context per turn, or carry forward previous context). For multi-turn, the standard approach is to reformulate the user's follow-up question into a standalone query before retrieval — otherwise the retriever sees "What about the exceptions?" without knowing which policy is being discussed. 7. The Long-Context Challenge: When Stuffing Beats Retrieval In February 2024, Google announced Gemini 1.5 Pro with a 1-million-token context window. By late 2025, Gemini 2.0 extended this to 2 million tokens. Claude supports 200K tokens. GPT-4o handles 128K. If your entire knowledge base fits in the context window, why retrieve at all? Just stuff everything in and let the model find what it needs. The empirical evidence is nuanced. Google's "Needle in a Haystack" tests show near-perfect recall across 1M tokens for simple factual lookups. But the "lost in the middle" phenomenon (Liu et al., 2023) persists: models attend more to the beginning and end of long contexts, with degraded attention to information in the middle. For complex reasoning over long contexts, accuracy drops significantly compared to the same information presented in a short, focused context. And then there's cost: processing 1M tokens through Gemini costs roughly 100x more than retrieving 5 relevant chunks and processing 2K tokens through a smaller model. Long context wins when: the corpus is small (under 200K tokens total), the task is simple factual lookup, latency matters more than cost, or the question requires holistic understanding of the entire corpus (summarisation, theme extraction). RAG wins when: the corpus exceeds any context window (100K+ documents), cost matters ($0.004/query vs. $0.40/query), specific citation is required, or the knowledge changes frequently and you can't afford to re-cache the entire context. The honest answer in 2026: for most enterprise use cases, the corpus is too large and changes too frequently for context stuffing to be practical. RAG is not being replaced by long context — it's being complemented by it. 8. The Agentic AI Challenge: When the Agent Subsumes Retrieval The more fundamental challenge to RAG comes not from longer context windows but from agentic AI architectures. An agent equipped with tool-use capabilities — web search, document reading, SQL queries, API calls, and computation — can dynamically decide what information it needs, fetch it in real time, verify it against multiple sources, and reason over it iteratively. This is fundamentally different from RAG's static pipeline, where retrieval happens once and the generator works with whatever was retrieved. Four paradigm shifts challenging RAG's default status: long-context models, agentic AI, fine-tuning, and graph-based retrieval. Each reduces RAG's necessity for specific use cases — but none eliminates it entirely. The Agentic RAG survey (Singh et al., arXiv 2501.09136, January 2025 — updated April 2026) provides the definitive taxonomy. Traditional RAG follows a fixed pipeline: query → retrieve → generate. Agentic RAG embeds autonomous agents into the pipeline, adding four capabilities: reflection (the agent evaluates whether the retrieved information is sufficient), planning (the agent decomposes complex queries into sub-queries), tool use (the agent can search the web, query databases, or call APIs alongside vector retrieval), and multi-agent collaboration (specialised agents handle different aspects of retrieval and generation). The key insight: in Agentic RAG, retrieval is one tool among many, not the only tool. Anthropic's Model Context Protocol (MCP), released in late 2024, formalises this: LLMs can connect to external tools — Google Drive, Slack, databases, web search — through a standardised protocol. An agent with MCP doesn't need a pre-built RAG pipeline to access documents; it can read them directly from their source systems. This doesn't eliminate the need for vector retrieval — MCP tools are slow for searching across 100K documents — but it changes the architecture from "build a RAG pipeline" to "give the agent access to tools and let it decide when to retrieve." Agentic RAG: the orchestrator agent decides per-query whether to retrieve from vectors, search the web, query structured data, traverse a knowledge graph, or use parametric memory — and can combine multiple sources in a single reasoning chain. 9. Self-RAG and Corrective RAG: The Model Decides When to Retrieve Self-RAG (Asai et al., arXiv 2023) introduced a paradigm shift: instead of always retrieving, the model learns to decide when retrieval is helpful and how to use retrieved information. The model is trained with special reflection tokens that trigger retrieval, evaluate the relevance of retrieved documents, and assess whether the generated response is supported by the evidence. This addresses the core problem of naive RAG: retrieving irrelevant information is worse than not retrieving at all. Corrective RAG (CRAG, Yan et al., arXiv 2401.15884, January 2024) takes this further. A lightweight retrieval evaluator (based on T5-large, 0.77B parameters) assesses the quality of retrieved documents and triggers three possible actions: Correct (documents are relevant → proceed with generation), Incorrect (documents are irrelevant → fall back to web search), or Ambiguous (partial relevance → combine corpus results with web search). A decompose-then-recompose algorithm selectively extracts key information from retrieved documents while filtering noise. CRAG demonstrated significant improvements over standard RAG on PopQA, Biography, PubHealth, and ARC-Challenge benchmarks. SCMRAG (AAMAS 2025) pushed further still, integrating a dynamic knowledge graph that evolves based on the system's interactions. When information is missing, SCMRAG autonomously generates a new retrieval query and searches external sources — making the retrieval system self-correcting and adaptive. This is where RAG meets agentic AI: the system doesn't just retrieve; it reasons about what to retrieve, evaluates what it found, and corrects its own mistakes. 10. Agentic RAG: Retrieval as One Tool Among Many The most important architectural evolution in 2026 is the transition from RAG-as-a-pipeline to RAG-as-a-tool. In a pipeline architecture, every query triggers the same sequence: embed → retrieve → rerank → generate. In an agentic architecture, the orchestrator agent decides per-query: should I retrieve from the vector store? Search the web? Query the SQL database? Use my parametric knowledge? Combine multiple sources? The retrieval system becomes one tool the agent can invoke — not the only tool. This is what the RAGFlow year-end review (December 2025) calls the evolution "from RAG to Context Engine" — where RAG's core capability is "intelligent retrieval" serving as the data foundation for agents. The retrieval component doesn't disappear; it becomes the knowledge layer that agents query when they need grounded, private, or structured information. The VentureBeat prediction that "contextual memory will surpass RAG for agentic AI" is accurate in one sense (agents manage their own state) but misleading in another (the memory system still needs a retrieval mechanism — it's just orchestrated differently). In practice, Agentic RAG in 2026 looks like this: a LangGraph or LlamaIndex agent with access to multiple tools — a vector retriever for internal documents, a web search tool for live data, a SQL tool for structured data, and a calculator for numerical reasoning. The agent's planning module decomposes complex queries into sub-tasks, routes each sub-task to the appropriate tool, and synthesises the results. This is more powerful than any single RAG pipeline — but it's also more complex to build, test, evaluate, and maintain. 11. Where RAG Remains Essential in 2026 Despite the paradigm shifts, RAG remains the right architecture for a specific — and large — set of production use cases. Enterprise scale: organisations with 100K+ documents, petabytes of unstructured data, and thousands of users cannot stuff their corpus into any context window. Amazon's internal documentation, a hospital's medical records, a law firm's case files — these exceed any model's context capacity. RAG scales to millions of documents with sub-second latency; context stuffing does not. Regulatory compliance and audit: In regulated industries — finance, healthcare, insurance, legal — the ability to attribute every claim in a generated response to a specific source document is non-negotiable. RAG provides provenance: "This answer was derived from Document X, Section 3, paragraph 2, last updated January 15, 2026." Pure generation cannot do this. Long-context stuffing can cite sources but with less precision. Under the EU AI Act's Article 13 transparency requirements for high-risk systems, source attribution is a compliance requirement, not a feature request. Freshness: Knowledge that changes daily — news, pricing, inventory levels, legal filings, security advisories — cannot be captured by fine-tuning (too slow to retrain) or long-context caching (too expensive to refresh). RAG's external knowledge store can be updated in minutes. A customer support system that answers questions about this week's product releases needs retrieval, not parametric memory. Cost: For high-volume applications (thousands of queries per hour), RAG + a small model (GPT-4o-mini, Claude Haiku) costs $0.001–0.01 per query. Context-stuffing 1M tokens through a frontier model costs $0.10–1.00 per query. At enterprise scale, this is a 100x cost difference. 12. Where RAG Is Being Replaced Simple Q&A over small document sets. If you have 50 product specification PDFs totalling 100K tokens, context stuffing with Gemini or Claude is simpler, cheaper, and more accurate than building a RAG pipeline. No chunking, no embedding, no vector database — just upload the documents and ask questions. For sub-200K-token corpora, this is now the default. Agentic workflows where the agent can search and verify. An agent with web search, MCP document access, and multi-step reasoning can answer questions about live data more accurately than a RAG pipeline with a static index. The agent verifies its answers across multiple sources; the RAG pipeline trusts whatever it retrieves. For questions like "What did the Fed announce yesterday?" an agent with web search is categorically better than RAG over a stale corpus. Tasks where the model already knows the answer. Asking a frontier model "What is gradient descent?" doesn't benefit from retrieval — the model's parametric knowledge is comprehensive and accurate for well-established topics. Retrieval adds 1–2 seconds of latency without improving quality. Low-stakes creative tasks. Drafting a marketing email, brainstorming product names, or writing a blog post outline — these don't need grounded retrieval. The model's creativity is the value; retrieval constraints reduce it. 13. The Framework Landscape RAG frameworks positioned by abstraction level (x-axis) and agentic capability (y-axis). Vector databases (bottom-left) provide storage. Frameworks (centre) provide orchestration. Managed services (right) provide end-to-end solutions. LangChain (Harrison Chase): The most widely adopted RAG framework, with LCEL for composable chains, LangGraph for agentic workflows, and LangSmith for evaluation/monitoring. Strengths: massive ecosystem, rapid feature development, extensive integrations. Weakness: historically unstable API, "framework tax" on debugging. LlamaIndex (Jerry Liu): Purpose-built for RAG with deep indexing strategies — tree, keyword, knowledge graph, vector, and hybrid indices. Stronger than LangChain for complex document structures; weaker on general agent orchestration. Haystack (deepset): Pipeline-based, production-focused, strong on hybrid search and European enterprise deployments. DSPy (Omar Khattab, Stanford): The "retrieval as programming" paradigm — compiles natural language signatures into optimised prompting strategies. Conceptually different from other frameworks; treats RAG as a programming problem, not an infrastructure problem. Managed services: Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases (fully managed RAG on AWS, integrated with S3 and OpenSearch), Azure AI Search + OpenAI (enterprise-grade, integrated with Microsoft 365), Google Vertex AI RAG (Gemini-native, supports 1M+ token context), Cohere RAG (optimised for Cohere's own embedding and reranking models), Vectara (end-to-end RAG API with built-in hallucination detection). The "RAG-as-a-feature" trend: RAG is being absorbed into model APIs themselves — Perplexity (search-augmented generation as a product), ChatGPT with browsing, Claude with web search and document tools. The standalone RAG pipeline is becoming an implementation detail rather than a product category. 14. RAG Evaluation: RAGAS and Beyond You cannot improve what you cannot measure. RAG evaluation requires metrics for both retrieval quality and generation quality — and the interaction between them. The RAGAS framework (Shahul Es et al., 2023) defines four core metrics that have become the industry standard. The four RAGAS metrics: Faithfulness (is the answer grounded in context?), Answer Relevance (does it address the question?), Context Precision (are retrieved chunks relevant?), Context Recall (did retrieval find everything needed?).
