Faithfulness: Is every claim in the generated answer supported by the retrieved context? This catches hallucination — the model inventing facts not present in the retrieved chunks. Answer Relevance: Does the answer actually address the user's question? This catches off-topic responses. Context Precision: Of the retrieved chunks, how many were actually relevant? This evaluates the retriever + reranker. Context Recall: Of all the information needed to answer the question, how much was retrieved? This catches cases where relevant documents exist in the corpus but weren't found. Additional tools: TruLens (traces, feedback functions), DeepEval (unit-test-style evaluation), LangSmith (LangChain-native tracing and evaluation). 15. GraphRAG: When Relationships Matter More Than Similarity Microsoft's GraphRAG (Edge et al., 2024) and the broader Graph RAG literature (surveyed in ACM Transactions on Information Systems, 2025) address a fundamental limitation of vector-based RAG: it retrieves by semantic similarity, which misses relational structure. Questions like "How does Department A's policy differ from Department B's?" or "What are the downstream effects of changing the travel reimbursement limit?" require understanding relationships between entities — not just finding similar text chunks. GraphRAG works by constructing a knowledge graph from the corpus (entity extraction + relationship extraction via LLM), applying community detection (Leiden algorithm) to identify clusters of related entities, and generating summaries at each community level. When a query arrives, the system retrieves not just similar chunks but the relevant subgraph — entities, relationships, and community summaries. This enables global queries ("Summarise the main themes across all policy documents") that vector RAG fundamentally cannot answer because it retrieves individual chunks, not corpus-level patterns. The tradeoff: GraphRAG is significantly more expensive to build (the indexing phase requires many LLM calls for entity/relationship extraction) and more complex to maintain (the knowledge graph must be updated when documents change). For simple factual Q&A, vector RAG is faster, cheaper, and good enough. For analytical, cross-document, or relationship-heavy queries, GraphRAG is superior. HybGRAG (ICLR 2025 submission) combines both: vector retrieval for factual queries, graph retrieval for relational queries, with a router that decides which to use per-query. 16. The Honest Verdict for 2026 The 2026 verdict: RAG is essential for large-scale, fresh, cost-sensitive, citation-requiring use cases. Long context wins for small corpora. Agentic wins for multi-step reasoning. Fine-tuning wins for stable, high-volume, latency-critical domains. RAG is not dead — but "vanilla, 2023-style RAG where you chunk documents, throw them in Pinecone, and call it a day" (to quote one industry observer) is absolutely dead. What survives and thrives is advanced RAG: hybrid search + cross-encoder reranking + query transformation + agentic orchestration + evaluation-driven iteration. The RAGFlow year-end review captures it well: RAG is evolving from "Retrieval-Augmented Generation" into a "Context Engine" — the intelligent retrieval layer that feeds agents, dashboards, and applications with the right information at the right time. The future is probably Agentic RAG: the agent orchestrates retrieval as one tool among many, deciding per-query whether to use vector search, web search, SQL, knowledge graphs, or parametric memory. The retrieval infrastructure doesn't disappear — it becomes the data backbone that agents depend on. For practitioners, this means: invest in retrieval quality (chunking, embedding, reranking, evaluation) over framework complexity. The frameworks will change; the quality of your retrieval will determine the quality of your AI system regardless of the orchestration layer on top. 17. Decision Framework: What to Build in 2026 Decision matrix: RAG vs. Long Context vs. Fine-Tuning vs. Agentic across 8 evaluation criteria. No single approach dominates all dimensions — the right choice depends on your specific constraints. ✓ Build RAG when: Corpus exceeds 1,000 documents or any model's context window. Knowledge changes daily or weekly (news, pricing, legal, inventory). Citations and source attribution are required (regulated industries). Cost per query must stay under $0.01 (high-volume applications). You need an audit trail for compliance (EU AI Act, GDPR, SOC2). Multiple user groups need different access controls over the same corpus. ✗ Don't build RAG when: Your corpus fits in a 200K-token context window → use long context directly. The task requires live, multi-source reasoning → use an agentic architecture. The knowledge is stable and well-established → fine-tune instead. The model already knows the answer from training → just ask it. You need creative generation, not factual grounding → retrieval constrains creativity. You don't have the engineering capacity to maintain chunking, embedding, and evaluation pipelines. 18. The People Behind the Paradigm Patrick Lewis, Ethan Perez, et al. (Meta AI / FAIR): The original RAG paper (NeurIPS 2020) that started it all. Lewis is now at Cohere; Perez is at Anthropic, working on AI safety. Vladimir Karpukhin (Meta): DPR (Dense Passage Retrieval), the retrieval backbone of the original RAG. Omar Khattab (Stanford → Databricks): ColBERT (late-interaction retrieval), ColBERTv2, and DSPy — the "retrieval as programming" paradigm that treats RAG as a compilation problem. Jerry Liu (LlamaIndex): Built the most RAG-specific framework, with deep indexing strategies that other frameworks lack. Harrison Chase (LangChain): Built the most widely adopted LLM framework, with LangGraph for agentic workflows and LangSmith for evaluation. Akari Asai (University of Washington): Self-RAG, the model that learns when to retrieve. Jia-Chen Gu (UCLA): CRAG, the corrective retrieval mechanism. Aditi Singh (Colorado State): The Agentic RAG survey that defined the 2025–2026 taxonomy. 19. Where RAG Goes Next Three trajectories are clear. First, RAG becomes invisible infrastructure — embedded into model APIs (Perplexity, ChatGPT browsing, Claude web search), cloud platforms (Bedrock, Vertex, Azure), and agent frameworks (LangGraph, LlamaIndex agents). Practitioners won't "build RAG pipelines"; they'll configure retrieval as a tool in an agentic system. The RAGFlow review calls this the evolution into a "Context Engine." Second, multimodal RAG matures — retrieving not just text but images, tables, charts, and video frames. ColPali (2024) demonstrated vision-language retrieval that indexes document images directly, bypassing text extraction entirely. By 2027, RAG pipelines that handle only text will be considered incomplete. Third, evaluation-driven RAG becomes standard practice. The gap between "we built a RAG system" and "we built a RAG system that works reliably" is measured by evaluation. RAGAS, TruLens, DeepEval, and LangSmith are making RAG evaluation as routine as model evaluation. The next frontier: continuous evaluation in production, where RAG quality metrics are monitored alongside model performance — and retrieval quality degrades trigger automatic reindexing, rechunking, or reranking threshold adjustments. RAG in 2026 is not a project you build and forget. It's a system you operate — and the organisations that treat it as operational infrastructure, not a one-time integration, will get the most value from it. ✅ End of notebook Cells 0–9 complete: setup → chunking → embedding → retrieval → RAG chain → Self-RAG pattern → agentic routing → evaluation → long-context comparison → decision router. Copy the green-bordered cells in order into a .ipynb for a working end-to-end example. Sources: Lewis et al. NeurIPS 2020 (original RAG); Karpukhin et al. 2020 (DPR); Asai et al. arXiv 2023 (Self-RAG); Yan et al. arXiv 2401.15884 2024 (CRAG); Singh et al. arXiv 2501.09136 Jan 2025, updated Apr 2026 (Agentic RAG Survey); GraphRAG ACM TOIS 2025; SCMRAG AAMAS 2025; Liu et al. 2023 (Lost in the Middle); Shahul Es et al. 2023 (RAGAS); Khattab Stanford (ColBERT, DSPy); RAGFlow year-end review Dec 2025; VentureBeat "6 data predictions for 2026." Frameworks: LangChain, LlamaIndex, Haystack, DSPy, RAGFlow, Vectara documentation. Managed: AWS Bedrock KB, Azure AI Search, Google Vertex RAG, Cohere RAG docs. LangChain 0.3+ · LlamaIndex 0.11+ · RAGAS · Chroma · 2024–2026 Article length determined by information surface area, not word count /div> /div>);}

docs.title: Guaranteed Prediction Intervals for Any Model: The Definitive Guide
docs.subtitle: Distribution-free, finite-sample coverage guarantees. No Gaussianity. No Bayesian priors. Works with any model.
docs.body: 1. The Uncertainty Problem in ML Your ML model predicts a number. The decision-maker asks: "How confident are you?" The model has no answer. Softmax probabilities are poorly calibrated — a classifier that says "87% confidence" is often wrong 30% of the time. Regression models give point predictions with no error bars. Bootstrap intervals are approximate and tend to undercover. Bayesian methods require specifying priors that are often wrong. The fundamental question — "what is the range of plausible values for this prediction?" — remains unanswered by standard ML. Conformal prediction solves this. It wraps around any model and produces prediction intervals (for regression) or prediction sets (for classification) with a mathematical guarantee: the true value is contained with probability at least 1−α. This guarantee holds for any distribution, any model, with finite samples. The only assumption is exchangeability — a condition weaker than i.i.d. that simply says the ordering of data points doesn't carry information. This article covers everything: the theory, the algorithms, the MAPIE library, and four production use cases. The conformal prediction guarantee. P(Y ∈ C(X)) ≥ 1−α for any distribution, any model, finite samples. Only requires exchangeability. 💡 Notebook Map Cells 0–9: Cell 0 Setup (§1) · Cell 1 Split Conformal (§3) · Cell 2 CQR (§4) · Cell 3 CV+ (§5) · Cell 4 Calibration Check (§7) · Cell 5 Classification Sets (§8) · Cell 6 Visualise (§9) · Cell 7 Compare Methods (§10) · Cell 8 Time Series (§11) · Cell 9 Production (§12) 2. Exchangeability: The Only Assumption Conformal prediction requires one assumption: exchangeability. A sequence of random variables Z₁, Z₂, ..., Zₙ is exchangeable if their joint distribution is invariant under any permutation. This is weaker than i.i.d.: i.i.d. data is always exchangeable, but exchangeable data need not be independent (e.g., sampling without replacement from a finite population is exchangeable but not independent). When does exchangeability fail? Time series — observations are ordered, autocorrelated, and potentially non-stationary. Direct application of conformal prediction to time series gives invalid coverage. Distribution shift — if the test distribution differs from the calibration distribution, the guarantee weakens. Feedback loops — if the model's predictions influence the data-generating process (e.g., a recommendation system), exchangeability breaks. For time series, adaptations exist: EnbPI (Xu & Xie 2021) uses ensemble diversity, ACI (Gibbs & Candès 2021) adjusts online. For distribution shift, robust conformal prediction (Cauchois et al. 2021) and Mondrian conformal provide partial solutions. The key insight: conformal prediction's coverage guarantee degrades gracefully — even when exchangeability is approximately violated, coverage is approximately correct. 3. Split Conformal Prediction The simplest and most practical variant. (1) Split data into training and calibration sets. (2) Train the model on the training set. (3) Compute nonconformity scores on the calibration set — typically the absolute residual sᵢ = |yᵢ − ŷᵢ|. (4) Compute the (1−α)(1+1/n)-quantile of the calibration scores as the interval half-width q. (5) For a new prediction: the interval is [ŷ − q, ŷ + q]. The guarantee: P(Y ∈ [ŷ−q, ŷ+q]) ≥ 1−α. Split conformal in 5 steps. The resulting interval has constant width — it doesn't adapt to local uncertainty. This is the main limitation, addressed by CQR. The constant-width limitation is significant for heteroscedastic data. If noise is low in one region and high in another, split conformal produces intervals that are too wide in the low-noise region (over-covering, wasteful) and potentially too narrow in the high-noise region (under-covering locally, though marginally correct). CQR solves this. 4. Conformalized Quantile Regression (CQR) CQR (Romano, Patterson, Candès, NeurIPS 2019) is the production standard. It trains quantile regression models for lower and upper bounds, then applies conformal calibration to guarantee coverage. The nonconformity score is: E(x,y) = max(q̂α/2(x) − y, y − q̂{1−α/2}(x)). This score measures how much the true value exceeds the predicted bounds — it's zero when y is inside the predicted interval and positive when it's outside. CQR = quantile regression (adaptive) + conformal calibration (guaranteed). The result: intervals that are narrower where the model is confident and wider where it's uncertain, with guaranteed coverage. The conformalization step adjusts the quantile predictions by a constant q (the quantile of calibration scores): [q̂α/2(x) − q, q̂{1−α/2}(x) + q]. This additive correction ensures the overall coverage is at least 1−α. If the quantile regression model is already well-calibrated, q will be small and the intervals change minimally. If the model miscalibrates, q corrects it. Either way, the guarantee holds. CQR inherits quantile regression's adaptiveness: intervals are wider in noisy regions and narrower in clean regions. And it inherits conformal prediction's guarantee: coverage is at least 1−α marginally. This combination is why CQR is the default method in MAPIE and the recommended approach for production prediction intervals. 5. CV+ and Jackknife+: No Data Waste Split conformal wastes the calibration set — data that can't be used for training. For small datasets, this matters. Jackknife+ (Barber et al., Annals of Statistics 2021) uses leave-one-out residuals: each training point gets a model trained without it. Coverage guarantee: P(Y ∈ C(X)) ≥ 1−2α/(n+1). CV+ is the K-fold version: K models trained on K-1 folds each, residuals computed on held-out folds. CV+ with 5-10 folds is the practical default. Three conformal variants trading off data efficiency against computation. CV+ (middle) is the production sweet spot. 6. Nonconformity Scores The score function determines interval shape. Absolute residual → constant width. CQR score → adaptive width. Classification score → prediction set size. The nonconformity score is the core abstraction. For regression, the absolute residual |y − ŷ| is the simplest score — it produces constant-width intervals. The CQR score max(q̂lo − y, y − q̂hi) produces adaptive intervals. Normalised residuals |y − ŷ| / σ̂(x) (where σ̂ is an estimated uncertainty) also produce adaptive intervals. For classification, 1 − f(x)y (one minus the softmax probability of the true class) produces prediction sets — smaller sets for confident predictions, larger sets for uncertain ones. The choice of score determines the quality of the intervals. Better scores produce tighter intervals with the same coverage guarantee. This is the main lever for improving conformal prediction performance: invest in better base models and better score functions, not in changing the conformal wrapper. 7. Calibration Verification Even though conformal prediction guarantees marginal coverage, you should verify empirically. The check is simple: compute the empirical coverage at multiple α levels and compare to the nominal coverage. A well-calibrated system shows empirical coverage ≥ nominal at every level. Additionally, check coverage per subgroup (gender, region, age bracket) to detect potential conditional coverage failures. 8. Prediction Sets for Classification For classification, conformal prediction produces prediction sets — sets of classes that contain the true class with guaranteed probability. The set size communicates confidence: a singleton set means the model is sure, a large set means it's uncertain. This is more actionable than a softmax probability — "the disease is either A or B" is a clear clinical instruction; "the probability of A is 0.55" is ambiguous. Prediction set sizes communicate model confidence. Singletons = confident. Pairs = ambiguous. Large sets = uncertain. The true class is in the set with probability ≥ 1−α. MAPIE implements three strategies: LAC (Least Ambiguous Classifier) — fixed threshold on softmax. APS (Adaptive Prediction Sets, Romano et al. 2020) — cumulative softmax threshold. RAPS (Regularized APS, Angelopoulos et al. 2021) — penalises large sets. RAPS is recommended for most applications because it produces the smallest sets while maintaining coverage. 9. Visualisation 10. Method Comparison Six UQ methods compared. Conformal prediction (top 3 rows) provides guaranteed coverage. Others provide approximate or conditional coverage. 11. Conformal Prediction for Time Series Time series violates exchangeability — observations are ordered and autocorrelated. Two solutions: EnbPI (Xu & Xie 2021) uses ensemble diversity to produce approximately valid intervals without exchangeability. ACI (Adaptive Conformal Inference, Gibbs & Candès 2021) adjusts the α threshold online, increasing it when the model under-covers and decreasing when it over-covers. EnCQR (Ensemble CQR, Feldman et al. 2023) combines CQR adaptiveness with ensemble exchangeability removal. MAPIE's MapieTimeSeriesRegressor implements EnbPI and ACI. For production time series, the recommended approach: engineer lag/rolling features (as tabular), train a gradient boosting model, and wrap with MAPIE using method="enbpi" for ensemble-based intervals. 12. Production Deployment MAPIE wraps any sklearn-compatible estimator. The production workflow: (1) train your model normally, (2) wrap with MapieRegressor or MapieQuantileRegressor, (3) call .fit(), (4) call .predict(X, alpha=0.1) to get predictions and intervals. The intervals are returned as a 3D array: (nsamples, 2, nalphalevels) — lower and upper bounds for each coverage level. For serving: the fitted MAPIE object can be pickled and loaded in a Flask/FastAPI endpoint. Each prediction returns both the point forecast and the interval bounds. The overhead compared to the base model is minimal — conformal prediction adds a constant (for split conformal) or a lookup (for CQR) to each prediction. ✅ End of notebook Cells 0–9: setup → split conformal → CQR → CV+ → calibration → classification sets → visualise → method comparison → time series → production. Copy green-bordered cells in order. 13. Use Case: Medical Diagnosis Prediction sets for disease classification: "the model says the condition is in the set {"{"}Condition A, Condition B{"}"} with 95% coverage." The clinician investigates both. A singleton set provides high confidence. A set of five conditions signals diagnostic uncertainty — order more tests. This is more actionable than "P(Condition A) = 0.55."' For patient risk scoring (regression), CQR intervals on clinical scores (NEWS2, SOFA) set alert thresholds: "alert if the upper bound of predicted SOFA exceeds 8." The coverage guarantee means the alarm has a known, calibrated false negative rate. 14. Use Case: Supply Chain Safety Stock Safety stock = upper prediction bound − point forecast. A 95% CQR interval with upper bound 238 and point forecast 215 means safety stock = 23 units. The coverage guarantee maps directly to service level: 95% coverage → 95% probability of meeting demand without stockout. CQR's adaptiveness means high-uncertainty products get more safety stock and low-uncertainty products get less — reducing total inventory cost while maintaining the service level target. 15. Use Case: Manufacturing Quality Control CQR intervals on dimensional measurements set inspection triggers. If the upper bound of a predicted dimension exceeds the specification tolerance, the part is flagged for inspection — even though the point prediction is within spec. The coverage guarantee means the false clear rate (parts that pass QC but are actually out of spec) is bounded at α. This connects conformal prediction to Six Sigma: the coverage level corresponds to the sigma level's confidence. 16. Limitations 1. Marginal, not conditional coverage. The guarantee is averaged over the distribution. Subgroups may have worse coverage. Workaround: Mondrian conformal, group-conditional. 2. Exchangeability for time series. Workaround: EnbPI, ACI. 3. Data waste (split). Workaround: CV+, jackknife+. 4. Constant width (basic conformal). Workaround: CQR for adaptive intervals. 5. No causal interpretation. Intervals quantify statistical uncertainty, not causal. 6. Sensitivity to base model. Better base models produce tighter intervals. Conformal prediction can't save a bad model — it just puts honest error bars on bad predictions. 17. The People Vladimir Vovk, Alex Gammerman, Glenn Shafer: the founders. "Algorithmic Learning in a Random World" (2005) laid the theoretical foundation. Yaniv Romano, Evan Patterson, Emmanuel Candès (Stanford): CQR (NeurIPS 2019), the paper that made conformal prediction practical. Rina Foygel Barber et al.: jackknife+ and CV+ (Annals of Statistics 2021). Anastasios Angelopoulos & Stephen Bates: the definitive tutorial (2023), RAPS, Learn-Then-Test framework. Isaac Gibbs & Emmanuel Candès: ACI (2021) for non-exchangeable data. Chen Xu & Yao Xie: EnbPI (2021) for time series. Thibault Cordier et al. (Quantmetry): MAPIE library (COPA 2023, scikit-learn-contrib). 18. Decision Framework ✓ Use conformal prediction when: You need prediction intervals or sets with formal coverage guarantees. Your model is any sklearn-compatible estimator (model-agnostic). You want calibrated uncertainty without Bayesian priors or distributional assumptions. Downstream decisions depend on uncertainty (safety stock, clinical alerts, risk). Regulatory requirements demand quantified uncertainty (medical devices, EU AI Act). You want adaptive intervals that reflect local uncertainty → use CQR. ✗ Consider alternatives when: You need the full predictive distribution → NGBoost, Bayesian, DeepAR. You need conditional coverage per subgroup → Mondrian or group-conditional. Your data has strong distribution shift → online conformal (ACI) or careful windowing. You need causal uncertainty → causal inference methods, not prediction intervals. Point predictions are sufficient and uncertainty isn't decision-relevant. Every prediction is incomplete without an uncertainty estimate. Conformal prediction makes this estimate rigorous: guaranteed coverage, distribution-free, model-agnostic, finite-sample. CQR makes it adaptive. MAPIE makes it practical. In 2026, shipping a prediction without a prediction interval is like publishing a scientific measurement without an error bar. Conformal prediction is how ML grows up. MAPIE: four modules for regression, classification, time series, and multi-label prediction with conformal guarantees. Sources: MAPIE docs (mapie.readthedocs.io), scikit-learn-contrib. Academic: Vovk, Gammerman, Shafer 2005; Romano, Patterson, Candès NeurIPS 2019 (CQR); Barber, Candès, Ramdas, Tibshirani 2021 (JK+/CV+); Angelopoulos & Bates 2023 (Tutorial, RAPS); Gibbs & Candès 2021 (ACI); Xu & Xie 2021 (EnbPI); Feldman et al. IEEE TNNLS 2023 (EnCQR); Cordier et al. COPA 2023 (MAPIE). Software: MAPIE, scikit-learn, crepes, nonconformist. MAPIE 1.3+ · CQR · Conformal Prediction Length determined by information, not word count /div> /div>);}

docs.title: Explaining Time Series Forecasts with Shapash: A Practitioner's Guide
docs.subtitle: How to use MAIF's explainability library to make lag features, rolling statistics, and calendar effects interpretable to business stakeholders — from feature engineering to production deployment.
docs.body: You've built a time series forecasting model. It uses LightGBM with 47 engineered features — lagged values, rolling means, day-of-week dummies, holiday indicators, temperature data. The MAPE is 4.2%, well within business requirements. The model is ready for production. And then your VP of Operations asks: "Why does the model think next Tuesday's demand will spike 18% above the rolling average?" This is the explainability gap. The model knows. You don't — or more precisely, you can't communicate what the model knows in language that a non-technical stakeholder can act on. Raw SHAP values tell you that lag1 contributed +0.32 and rolling7dmean contributed -0.18, but what your VP needs to hear is: "Yesterday's value was unusually high, and the 7-day trend has been climbing, which together push Tuesday's forecast up. The biggest single driver is the previous day's reading." Shapash, the open-source Python library developed by MAIF (a French mutual insurer), bridges this gap. It wraps SHAP and LIME computations in a presentation layer that performs inverse encoding (translating feature17 back into "Neighbourhood: Northridge"), generates interactive dashboards, and produces standalone HTML audit reports. For time series forecasting, the key insight is that Shapash doesn't need to know your data is temporal — it explains the tabular features that your forecasting pipeline produces. The temporal intelligence lives in your feature engineering; Shapash explains the result. Before diving into the implementation, three concepts underpin everything Shapash does. A Shapley value answers: *how much did feature i contribute to this specific prediction, relative to the average prediction? It comes from cooperative game theory (Lloyd Shapley, 1953; Nobel Prize 2012). The idea: consider every possible subset of features ("coalition"). For each, measure how much the prediction changes when you add feature i. The Shapley value is the weighted average of these marginal contributions across all coalitions. It is the only attribution method that simultaneously satisfies local accuracy (contributions sum to the prediction minus the base value), missingness (missing features contribute zero), and consistency (if a feature's marginal contribution increases in a new model, its attribution doesn't decrease). Computing exact Shapley values is exponential in the number of features (2^n^ coalitions). TreeSHAP, by Scott Lundberg (2018), exploits the tree structure of gradient-boosted models to compute exact Shapley values in polynomial time — O(TLD²) where T is the number of trees, L the maximum leaves, and D the maximum depth. This is why Shapash works best with XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost, and scikit-learn tree ensembles: the SHAP backend can compute exact values fast enough for interactive exploration. Most ML pipelines encode categorical features before training. An ordinal encoder maps "Monday" → 0, "Tuesday" → 1, etc. A one-hot encoder creates dowMonday, dowTuesday, ... When SHAP computes contributions, it operates on these encoded features. Shapash reverses the encoding at display time, showing "Day of Week: Monday contributed +$5k" instead of "feature23 = 0 contributed +0.023." For time series, this means your lag features, rolling statistics, and calendar dummies all appear with human-readable labels in the dashboard and report. The critical step is feature engineering — converting the raw time series into a tabular dataset where each row represents a forecast origin and each column represents a feature that Shapash can explain. 💡 Why this works for Shapash Shapash doesn't need temporal awareness — it explains tabular features. The temporal structure is encoded in the features (lags carry history, rolling stats carry trend, calendar features carry seasonality). When Shapash says "lag1 contributed +$18k," it means the model relied heavily on yesterday's value for this forecast. When it says "month = January contributed -$8k," it means the model learned a seasonal pattern. The feature engineering is your domain knowledge; Shapash makes it visible. ⚠️ Temporal split matters Always use shuffle=False for time series. Shuffled splits leak future information into training, inflating accuracy and producing misleading SHAP explanations — the model "explains" patterns it shouldn't have seen. The runapp() call launches a Dash-based web application on localhost. The dashboard provides four linked views: global feature importance (which features matter most across all forecasts), feature contribution plots (how a single feature's values relate to its SHAP contributions), local explanations (why a specific forecast came out the way it did), and a filterable data table that lets you select subsets by feature values, prediction ranges, or error magnitudes. Crucially, every label in the dashboard uses the featuresdict you provided — so your VP sees "Yesterday's Value" and "7-Day Rolling Average," not "lag1" and "rolling7dmean." This generates a standalone HTML file — no server required — containing project metadata, dataset descriptions, feature distributions, model performance metrics, global and local explainability visualisations, and correlation analyses. It is designed to be emailed to compliance, shared with auditors, or archived as regulatory documentation. ✅ SmartPredictor vs. SmartExplainer SmartExplainer is for exploration — it holds the full test set, all SHAP values, and the web app. SmartPredictor is for production — it's lighter, runs per-instance, includes consistency checks, and outputs summarised explanations in configurable natural language. Use SmartExplainer during development; deploy SmartPredictor. Consider a utility company forecasting hourly electricity demand. The model uses 28 features: 7 lag features (hours -1 through -24 plus -168 for same-hour-last-week), 4 rolling statistics (6h, 12h, 24h, 168h means), 6 calendar features (hour, day of week, month, holiday, daylight hours, working day flag), and 11 weather features (temperature, humidity, wind speed, cloud cover — current and 3 lagged values). The model is LightGBM with 800 trees. Shapash reveals patterns that raw accuracy metrics hide. Globally, the dashboard shows that lag1 (previous hour's demand) and temperature dominate — expected for energy. But the contribution plot for hourofday reveals that the model has learned a non-monotonic relationship: demand peaks at hours 8–9 and 18–20 (morning and evening peaks), with a trough at 14:00. This pattern matches known load curves but was never explicitly engineered — the model discovered it from the data. Locally, when the model forecasts an unusual spike on a Tuesday evening, the SmartExplainer shows: "Temperature: -2°C (+3.4 MW above average), Previous Hour: 42 MW (+2.1 MW), Hour of Day: 19:00 (+1.8 MW)." The operations team doesn't need to understand gradient boosting — they can see that the cold snap is driving the spike, confirm that it makes physical sense, and decide whether to activate additional generation capacity. The audit report, generated at model deployment, documents the training data period (2021–2024), the feature set, the model's MAE (1.2 MW) and MAPE (2.8%), and the full global explainability analysis. When the energy regulator asks how automated dispatch decisions are made, the utility hands over the HTML file. A retail chain forecasts weekly sales per store. Features include: 4 lag features (weeks -1, -2, -4, -52), rolling 4-week and 12-week means, promotion flags, store-level categorical features (region, format, size tier), and macroeconomic indicators (CPI, consumer confidence). The model is XGBoost. Shapash's feature grouping capability (available since v2.3) is valuable here: you can group the four lag features under "Recent Sales History" and the two macroeconomic features under "Economic Conditions," collapsing the dashboard to a level of abstraction that a regional manager can parse. The filter functionality in the web app lets the merchandising team select only stores in the "Urban Large" format and see how feature importance shifts — perhaps promotions matter more in urban locations, while weather matters more in suburban ones. The SmartPredictor, deployed in the weekly batch pipeline, appends a one-line explanation to each store's forecast: "Store 247 forecast: $182k (+12% vs. baseline). Top drivers: Last Week's Sales (+$14k), Active Promotion (+$8k), Week 52 Seasonality (-$3k)." The store manager reads this alongside the number and knows whether the spike is promotion-driven (temporary) or trend-driven (sustained) — a distinction that changes the replenishment decision. Shapash has real constraints that practitioners must understand. No native deep learning support. If your forecasting model is an LSTM, Transformer, or N-BEATS architecture, Shapash's TreeSHAP backend won't work. You can provide pre-computed SHAP values (via DeepSHAP or KernelSHAP), but the web app and report are optimised for tabular feature-level explanations, not sequence-level attributions. For deep TS models, consider TsSHAP, TimeSHAP, or C-SHAP as complementary tools. Feature independence assumption. Shapley values assume features are independent — but in time series, lag features are highly correlated by construction (lag1 and lag2 are adjacent values of the same series). This can redistribute attribution between correlated features in unstable ways. Use Shapash's Stability metric to detect this: if the same prediction gets very different explanations on slightly perturbed data, your features are too correlated for reliable local attribution. Memory and scale. SmartExplainer loads all SHAP values into memory. For datasets with millions of rows, use the sample parameter or compute SHAP on a representative subset. The web app defaults to 1,000 rows for responsiveness. Multi-step forecasting. If your model produces multi-horizon forecasts (predicting t+1 through t+7 simultaneously), you need to explain each horizon separately. Shapash treats each target as a single regression output. Shapash doesn't make your time series model explainable. Your feature engineering does that. Shapash makes the explanation visible* — to the people who need to trust it, act on it, audit it, and regulate it. In a world where the EU AI Act requires that high-risk AI systems be "sufficiently transparent for deployers to interpret the system's output," that visibility is not optional. It is the product.

docs.title: Beyond Point Forecasts: Prediction Intervals That Actually Work
docs.subtitle: Quantile regression for adaptiveness. Conformal prediction for guarantees. CQR for both. MAPIE for production.
docs.body: 1. Why Point Forecasts Are Not Enough Your demand forecasting model predicts 215 units for next Tuesday. The supply chain team orders 215 units. Actual demand is 238. You stockout, losing $23k in revenue. The next week, the model predicts 220. The team orders 235 "just in case." Actual demand is 198. You overstock, incurring $15k in holding costs. The problem isn't the model's accuracy — it's the absence of uncertainty. A probabilistic forecast — "215 units, 90% prediction interval [195, 238]" — lets the team set safety stock at the 95th percentile, balancing stockout risk against holding cost. This article covers the complete landscape: why point forecasts mislead decision-makers; quantile regression for estimating specific percentiles; conformal prediction for distribution-free coverage guarantees; CQR (Romano et al., NeurIPS 2019) for combining both; MAPIE as the production library; calibration assessment; and four use cases where prediction intervals change the economics. Point forecast (left) gives a single number with no confidence. Probabilistic forecast (right) gives an interval that quantifies uncertainty and enables optimal decision-making. 💡 Notebook Map Cells 0–9: Cell 0 Setup (§1) · Cell 1 Quantile Regression (§3) · Cell 2 Split Conformal (§4) · Cell 3 MAPIE CQR (§5) · Cell 4 Calibration Check (§7) · Cell 5 CV+ / Jackknife+ (§6) · Cell 6 Classification Sets (§9) · Cell 7 Time Series CP (§10) · Cell 8 Visualise Intervals (§8) · Cell 9 Safety Stock (§11) 2. Prediction Intervals vs. Confidence Intervals Practitioners constantly confuse these. A confidence interval quantifies uncertainty about a parameter (e.g., the population mean). A prediction interval quantifies uncertainty about a future observation. Prediction intervals are always wider because they include both parameter uncertainty AND irreducible noise. When the supply chain team asks "what's the range of demand?" they want a prediction interval, not a confidence interval. Using the wrong one leads to systematically narrow intervals and more stockouts than expected. For ML models, the standard prediction interval from linear regression (ŷ ± t·se·√(1 + 1/n + ...)) doesn't apply — it assumes normality, homoscedasticity, and a linear model. ML models violate all three. This is why conformal prediction matters: it provides valid prediction intervals for ANY model with NO distributional assumptions. 3. Quantile Regression Quantile regression directly predicts specific percentiles of the conditional distribution. Instead of minimising mean squared error (which estimates the conditional mean), it minimises the pinball loss: Lτ(y, q) = τ·max(y−q, 0) + (1−τ)·max(q−y, 0), where τ is the target quantile. For τ=0.5, this gives the median. For τ=0.05 and τ=0.95, it gives a 90% prediction interval. The pinball loss is asymmetric: for τ=0.95, under-predictions (y much greater than q) are penalised 19x more than over-predictions. This pushes the model to predict the upper tail. LightGBM, XGBoost, and CatBoost all support quantile regression natively (objective="quantile"). You train three models — one for each quantile — to produce a prediction interval. The interval adapts to local uncertainty: wider in regions where the data is noisy, narrower where it's clean. But there is no coverage guarantee — the intervals may systematically under-cover or over-cover. Quantile crossing (the 95th percentile prediction is lower than the 5th) can also occur when models are trained independently. 4. Split Conformal Prediction Conformal prediction provides what quantile regression cannot: a finite-sample, distribution-free coverage guarantee. The guarantee is: P(Y ∈ C(X)) ≥ 1−α for any distribution, any model, with finite samples. The only assumption is exchangeability — data points are exchangeable (weaker than i.i.d.; order doesn't matter). No Gaussianity. No correct model specification. No asymptotic approximation. Split conformal in 5 steps. The coverage guarantee P(Y ∈ interval) ≥ 1−α holds for ANY distribution and ANY model. Only assumption: exchangeability. Split conformal is the simplest variant: (1) split data into training and calibration sets, (2) train on training data, (3) compute nonconformity scores on calibration data (typically |y − ŷ|), (4) take the (1−α) quantile of scores as the interval half-width q, (5) for new predictions: ŷ ± q. The interval has constant width — it doesn't adapt to local uncertainty. This is the main limitation: regions of low uncertainty get intervals that are too wide, and regions of high uncertainty get intervals that may be too narrow. 5. Conformalized Quantile Regression (CQR) CQR (Romano, Patterson, Candès, NeurIPS 2019) combines the best of both worlds: the adaptiveness of quantile regression with the coverage guarantee of conformal prediction. It trains quantile regression models for the lower and upper bounds, then calibrates them using conformal prediction to ensure the coverage guarantee holds. The nonconformity score is: E(x,y) = max(q̂α/2(x) − y, y − q̂_{1−α/2}(x)) — how much the true value exceeds the predicted bounds. CQR = quantile regression (adaptive width) + conformal calibration (coverage guarantee). The result: intervals that are wider where the model is uncertain and narrower where it's confident, with guaranteed coverage. CQR inherits the adaptiveness of quantile regression: intervals are wider in high-variance regions and narrower in low-variance regions. But unlike raw quantile regression, the conformal calibration step guarantees that the overall coverage is at least 1−α. This makes CQR the current production standard for prediction intervals in ML: it's the method MAPIE defaults to for regression, and it works with any base model (gradient boosting, random forest, neural network). The key limitation: the coverage guarantee is marginal (averaged over the data distribution), not conditional (for every subgroup). CQR may under-cover in specific subpopulations while over-covering in others, as long as the average is correct. Mondrian conformal prediction and group-conditional methods address this for fairness-sensitive applications. 6. CV+ and Jackknife+: No Data Waste Split conformal wastes data — the calibration set can't be used for training. For small datasets, this matters. Jackknife+ (Barber et al., Annals of Statistics 2021) uses leave-one-out residuals as nonconformity scores: each training point gets a model trained without it, and the residual becomes its calibration score. Coverage guarantee: P(Y ∈ C(X)) ≥ 1−2α/(n+1). CV+ is the cross-validation analogue: K-fold CV residuals instead of LOO. Both use all data for both training and calibration, but require K+1 model fits instead of 1. MAPIE implements both via the cv parameter: cv="prefit" for split conformal, cv=10 for CV+ with 10 folds, cv=-1 for jackknife+ (LOO). For most production use cases, CV+ with 5–10 folds provides the best tradeoff between data efficiency and computational cost. 7. Calibration: Does 90% Really Mean 90%? A prediction interval is only useful if its nominal coverage matches its empirical coverage. A "90% interval" that contains the true value only 75% of the time gives false confidence. A "90% interval" that covers 99% of the time is too conservative — the intervals are wider than necessary, leading to wasteful decisions. Three calibration states. Well-calibrated: nominal = empirical. Under-covering: intervals too narrow. Over-covering: intervals too wide. How to check: (1) Empirical coverage — simply count what fraction of test observations fall within their intervals. (2) CRPS (Continuous Ranked Probability Score, Gneiting & Raftery 2007) — a proper scoring rule that evaluates the full predictive distribution. (3) Winkler score — penalises both width and miscoverage. (4) PIT histogram (Probability Integral Transform) — should be uniform for well-calibrated probabilistic forecasts. How to fix miscalibration: conformal prediction is itself a calibration procedure — applying conformal prediction on top of any base model (even a poorly calibrated one) produces guaranteed coverage. This is why CQR works: even if the quantile regression models are miscalibrated, the conformal calibration step corrects them. 8. Visualisation Always plot prediction intervals as shaded bands, not just point forecasts. For regression: x-axis = feature or time, y-axis = target, with the shaded band showing the PI and the line showing the point forecast. For time series: the fan chart (increasingly wide bands for longer horizons). The visual immediately communicates where the model is confident (narrow band) and where it's uncertain (wide band). 9. Prediction Sets for Classification For classification, conformal prediction produces prediction sets — sets of classes that contain the true class with guaranteed probability. Instead of "the model says Class A with 87% probability," conformal prediction says "the model says the true class is in the set {"{"}A, B{"}"} with 90% coverage." Smaller sets are more informative; a set containing all classes is uninformative. MAPIE implements three strategies: LAC (Least Ambiguous Classifier) — fixed threshold on softmax scores. APS (Adaptive Prediction Sets, Romano et al. 2020) — adapts set size based on model uncertainty. RAPS (Regularized APS) — penalises large sets to keep them small. For most applications, APS or RAPS is preferred. 10. Conformal Prediction for Time Series Time series data breaks the exchangeability assumption — observations are ordered and autocorrelated. Direct application of conformal prediction to time series produces invalid coverage. Two main solutions exist: EnbPI (Ensemble Batch Prediction Intervals, Xu & Xie 2021) uses ensemble diversity instead of exchangeability, producing approximately valid intervals. ACI (Adaptive Conformal Inference, Gibbs & Candès 2021) adjusts the coverage threshold online as distribution shift occurs. MAPIE's MapieTimeSeriesRegressor implements both. EnCQR (Ensemble Conformalized Quantile Regression, Feldman et al., IEEE TNNLS 2023) combines CQR with bootstrap ensembles, removing the exchangeability requirement while preserving adaptiveness. This is currently the strongest method for time series prediction intervals — valid, adaptive, and practical. 11. Use Case: Supply Chain Safety Stock The direct connection between probabilistic forecasts and inventory management: safety stock = upper quantile − point forecast. A 95% prediction interval upper bound of 238 units with a point forecast of 215 means safety stock = 23 units. This directly maps the model's uncertainty to a dollar cost. The service level — the probability of meeting demand without stockout — equals the prediction interval coverage level. A 95% PI → 95% service level. Without prediction intervals, safety stock is set by heuristic (e.g., "order 120% of the point forecast") or by historical variance. With conformal prediction intervals, safety stock is set optimally: it adapts per product and per period based on the model's uncertainty. Products the model is confident about get small safety stocks; products with high uncertainty get large safety stocks. This reduces total inventory cost while maintaining the target service level. ✅ End of notebook Cells 0–9: setup → quantile regression → split conformal → CQR → calibration → CV+ → classification sets → time series CP → visualisation → safety stock. Copy green-bordered cells in order. 12. Use Case: Energy Trading Energy trading requires probabilistic price and load forecasts for risk-adjusted bidding. A wind farm operator bidding into the day-ahead market needs to know not just the expected generation (45 MW) but the range of outcomes (90% PI: [28, 62] MW). Bidding the point forecast and under-delivering incurs imbalance penalties. Bidding the lower bound is safe but leaves revenue on the table. The optimal bid depends on the asymmetry of penalties — which requires the full predictive distribution, not just the mean. CQR is particularly well-suited: the intervals adapt to weather uncertainty (wide on cloudy days, narrow on sunny days) while maintaining the coverage guarantee that risk management requires. The coverage level maps directly to the Value at Risk (VaR) framework used in energy finance. 13. Use Case: Medical Prediction In clinical decision support, prediction sets for classification communicate diagnostic uncertainty directly. A model predicting disease type outputs the set {"{"}Condition A, Condition B{"}"} at 95% coverage — the clinician investigates both. A singleton set {"{"}Condition A{"}"} gives high confidence. A set containing five conditions signals that the model can't distinguish and more diagnostic tests are needed. This is more actionable than a softmax probability of 0.67 — which most clinicians can't calibrate against. For patient deterioration risk (regression), conformal prediction intervals on clinical scores (NEWS2, SOFA) let clinicians set alert thresholds: "alert if the upper bound of the predicted SOFA score exceeds 8." The coverage guarantee means the alarm has a known false negative rate — critical for patient safety. 14. The Competitive Landscape Methods positioned by coverage guarantee strength (x) and adaptiveness (y). CQR dominates the upper-right: strong guarantees AND adaptive intervals. Six methods compared across coverage guarantee, adaptiveness, assumptions, and cost. CQR vs. raw quantile regression: CQR adds conformal calibration on top, converting "intervals that are probably about right" into "intervals with guaranteed coverage." The cost: a calibration set holdout. The benefit: you can state "90% coverage" with mathematical certainty. CQR vs. Bayesian methods: Bayesian gives richer distributional information (full posterior predictive) but requires correct priors and is computationally expensive. CQR is prior-free, model-agnostic, and computationally cheap. CQR vs. bootstrap: bootstrap intervals tend to undercover (are too narrow) and require N retraining runs. CQR has finite-sample guarantees and requires no retraining. 15. Limitations and Workarounds 1. Marginal vs. conditional coverage. Conformal guarantees are marginal (averaged over the data distribution). Specific subgroups may have worse coverage. Workaround: Mondrian conformal prediction, group-conditional conformal, or checking coverage per subgroup. 2. Exchangeability for time series. Direct conformal breaks on temporal data. Workaround: EnbPI, ACI, or sliding-window conformal. 3. Data waste in split conformal. The calibration set can't train. Workaround: CV+ or jackknife+. 4. Interval width vs. coverage tradeoff. Narrower intervals have lower coverage. Workaround: use CQR for adaptive width, or accept wider intervals for critical applications. 5. No causal interpretation. Prediction intervals quantify statistical uncertainty, not causal uncertainty. They don't answer "what if we change X?"' 16. The People Vladimir Vovk, Alex Gammerman, Glenn Shafer — founders of conformal prediction ("Algorithmic Learning in a Random World," 2005). Yaniv Romano, Evan Patterson, Emmanuel Candès (Stanford) — CQR (NeurIPS 2019), the paper that made conformal prediction practical for ML. Rina Foygel Barber et al. — jackknife+ and CV+ (Annals of Statistics 2021). Anastasios Angelopoulos & Stephen Bates — the definitive tutorial on conformal prediction (2023), learn-then-test framework. Thibault Cordier et al. (Quantmetry) — MAPIE library (scikit-learn-contrib, COPA 2023). Tony Duan et al. (Stanford) — NGBoost (2020), natural gradient boosting for distributional regression. Tilmann Gneiting & Adrian Raftery — proper scoring rules and calibration (JASA 2007). 17. Where Probabilistic Forecasting Is Heading Three directions: (1) Conformal prediction for LLMs — applying prediction sets to language model outputs (MAPIE 2026 roadmap includes LLM-as-Judge risk control). (2) Conditional coverage — methods that guarantee coverage for every subgroup, not just marginally. The arXiv 2501.17520 (Jan 2026) paper on double-robust conditional feature importance contributes to this direction. (3) Online conformal prediction — methods that maintain coverage guarantees under distribution shift without requiring data storage. ACI (Gibbs & Candès 2021) is the foundation; production implementations are maturing in MAPIE. 18. Decision Framework ✓ Use conformal prediction (CQR/MAPIE) when: You need prediction intervals with formal coverage guarantees. Your model is any sklearn-compatible estimator (model-agnostic). The data is exchangeable (or time series with EnbPI/ACI adaptation). You need calibrated uncertainty for downstream decisions (safety stock, risk). You want adaptive intervals that reflect local uncertainty (use CQR). Regulatory requirements demand quantified uncertainty (EU AI Act, medical devices). ✗ Consider alternatives when: You need the full predictive distribution, not just intervals → NGBoost, Bayesian, DeepAR. You need causal uncertainty ("what if") → causal inference methods. You need conditional coverage per subgroup → Mondrian or group-conditional methods. Your data has strong distribution shift → online methods or careful window selection. You need point predictions only → standard regression (but consider adding intervals anyway). Every point forecast is a lie by omission — it omits how wrong it might be. Prediction intervals tell the decision-maker: "I predict 215, but I could be off by as much as 23 in either direction." Conformal prediction makes this statement mathematically rigorous. CQR makes it adaptive. MAPIE makes it practical. In 2026, shipping a prediction without a prediction interval is like shipping a measurement without an error bar — technically possible, but professionally irresponsible. Sources: MAPIE docs (mapie.readthedocs.io), scikit-learn-contrib. Academic: Vovk, Gammerman, Shafer 2005 (Conformal Prediction); Romano, Patterson, Candès NeurIPS 2019 (CQR); Barber, Candès, Ramdas, Tibshirani, Annals of Statistics 2021 (Jackknife+/CV+); Angelopoulos & Bates 2023 (Tutorial); Gneiting & Raftery JASA 2007 (Scoring Rules); Duan et al. 2020 (NGBoost); Xu & Xie 2021 (EnbPI); Gibbs & Candès 2021 (ACI); Cordier et al. COPA 2023 (MAPIE); Feldman et al. IEEE TNNLS 2023 (EnCQR). Software: MAPIE, scikit-learn, LightGBM quantile, NeuralProphet, GluonTS. MAPIE 1.3+ · scikit-learn · CQR · Conformal Prediction Length determined by information, not word count /div> /div>);}

docs.title: The Monotonic Constraint Trick That Prevents Nonsensical Predictions
docs.subtitle: How one XGBoost parameter can enforce domain knowledge and eliminate predictions that violate common sense
docs.body: How one XGBoost parameter can enforce domain knowledge and eliminate predictions that violate common sense The Problem You've built a house price model. It's got great RMSE. Then your stakeholder points out: "Why does your model predict a lower price when I add a bedroom?" This happens more often than you'd think. Tree-based models can learn non-monotonic relationships from noisy data — meaning more bedrooms might decrease predicted price in certain feature interaction regions. The model is technically correct (it fits the noise), but it's practically wrong (it violates domain knowledge). Monotonic constraints fix this in one line of code. How Monotonic Constraints Work The constraint tells the model: "I don't care what the data says — the partial dependence of price on bedrooms must NEVER decrease." The model can still learn complex interactions with other features. It just can't violate the monotonic rule for the constrained feature. The Mechanics At every split point, XGBoost checks: "If I split here, will the left child have a higher prediction than the right child for an increasing constraint?" If yes, that split is forbidden. Key Insight: Monotonic constraints don't just clip predictions — they change the tree structure itself. The model learns a fundamentally different (and more sensible) function. Code Example Output: The monotonic model has virtually identical accuracy — but its predictions always make sense. Proof That It Works Output: The unconstrained model has a price drop at 4 bedrooms (marked ↓ !!!). The constrained model is strictly increasing. Constraint Options at a Glance | Value | Meaning | Example Feature | |-------|---------|-----------------| | 1 | Must be increasing | bedrooms → price | | -1 | Must be decreasing | age → price | | 0 | No constraint (default) | color, zip code | Supported Libraries | Library | Parameter | Format | |---------|-----------|--------| | XGBoost | monotoneconstraints | Tuple: (1, 0, -1) | | LightGBM | monotoneconstraints | List: [1, 0, -1] | | CatBoost | monotoneconstraints | Dict: {0: 1, 2: -1} | | Scikit-learn | monotoniccst (v1.4+) | Array: [1, 0, -1] | Key Insight: As of scikit-learn 1.4, HistGradientBoostingRegressor and HistGradientBoostingClassifier also support monotonic constraints via the monotoniccst parameter. You're no longer locked into XGBoost/LightGBM. When to Use This Ideal use cases: Pricing models — price should increase with size, quality, quantity Credit scoring — higher income should never decrease creditworthiness Insurance — more coverage should not decrease premium When NOT to use: Truly non-monotonic relationships — temperature vs. crop yield has a peak Exploratory analysis — you're still discovering relationships in the data When domain knowledge is uncertain — wrong constraints will hurt accuracy TL;DR Cheat Sheet What: Monotonic constraints force tree-based models to respect "more X → more/less Y" rules Why: Prevents nonsensical predictions where adding bedrooms decreases price How: monotoneconstraints=(1, 0, -1) — one param, per feature Cost: Typically <1% accuracy loss for massive interpretability gain Works in: XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost, scikit-learn 1.4+ Rule of thumb: If a stakeholder would call a prediction "obviously wrong," you need a constraint Gotcha: Only enforces marginal monotonicity — feature interactions can still be complex Further Reading XGBoost Monotonic Constraints docs LightGBM Monotone Constraints Scikit-learn 1.4 release notes — monotonic_cst parameter
